BHARATDEFENCE

All Categories • One Syllabus

Written Exam Syllabus & Strategy

A combined, subject-wise syllabus covering every Armed Forces category with a written exam — NDA, CDS, AFCAT, INET, Agniveer (Army/Navy/Air Force), SSC, CAPF and TGC. Topics, must-know formulas and a focused preparation strategy, all in one place.

NDA CDS AFCAT INET Agniveer (GD/Tech/Clerk/Navy/Vayu) SSC (Tech/Non-Tech) CAPF AC TGC
Topics Covered
  • Algebra — sets, equations, complex numbers, sequence & series, binomial theorem
  • Trigonometry — ratios, identities, heights & distances, inverse functions
  • Geometry & Mensuration — lines, circles, conic sections, 2D/3D mensuration
  • Vectors & Matrices — algebra of vectors, matrix operations, determinants
  • Differential Calculus — limits, continuity, derivatives, maxima/minima
  • Integral Calculus & Differential Equations — definite/indefinite integrals, area under curves
  • Statistics & Probability — mean, median, mode, standard deviation, probability theorems
  • Number System & Simplification — HCF/LCM, ratio-proportion, percentage, profit-loss, time-speed-distance (Agniveer/SSC level)
🧮 Must-Know Formulas
  • Quadratic roots: x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac)) / 2a
  • sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ, 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ
  • Area of triangle = ½ × base × height; Heron's: √[s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c)]
  • Circle: Area = πr², Circumference = 2πr
  • Sphere: Volume = (4/3)πr³, Surface Area = 4πr²
  • Cylinder: Volume = πr²h, Cone: Volume = (1/3)πr²h
  • nth term of AP: aₙ = a + (n−1)d; Sum: Sₙ = n/2 [2a + (n−1)d]
  • nth term of GP: aₙ = arⁿ⁻¹; Sum (r≠1): Sₙ = a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1)
  • Distance formula: √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²]
  • Permutations: ⁿPᵣ = n!/(n−r)! | Combinations: ⁿCᵣ = n!/[r!(n−r)!]
  • Simple Interest = (P×R×T)/100; Compound Interest = P(1+R/100)ⁿ − P
  • Speed = Distance/Time; Average Speed = 2xy/(x+y) for equal distances
Topics Covered
  • Grammar — tenses, articles, prepositions, conjunctions, voice (active/passive), narration (direct/indirect)
  • Vocabulary — synonyms, antonyms, one-word substitution, idioms & phrases
  • Error Detection / Spotting Errors
  • Sentence Improvement & Rearrangement (jumbled sentences)
  • Reading Comprehension passages
  • Fill in the blanks / cloze test
  • Antonyms & synonyms in context, spelling correction
📘 High-Yield Rules
  • Subject-Verb Agreement: singular subject → singular verb, plural subject → plural verb
  • Passive Voice formula: Object + be(am/is/are/was/were) + V3 + by + Subject
  • Direct → Indirect: present simple → past simple; "today"→"that day"; "tomorrow"→"the next day"
  • Conditional sentences: If + present → future (Type 1); If + past → would (Type 2)
Topics Covered
  • History — Ancient, Medieval, Modern India, Freedom Struggle
  • Geography — Physical, Indian & World Geography, climate, rivers, resources
  • Indian Polity — Constitution, Fundamental Rights/Duties, Parliament, judiciary
  • Economy — Basic concepts, Five Year Plans, budget, banking, GST
  • General Science — Physics, Chemistry, Biology (school level concepts)
  • Current Affairs — national & international events, defence news, sports, awards
  • Defence & Security — wars, operations, organisations (UN, SAARC, BRICS), defence schemes
🔬 Key Science Formulas
  • Speed = Distance / Time; Force (F) = mass × acceleration (F = ma)
  • Work = Force × Displacement; Power = Work / Time
  • Newton's Second Law: F = ma; Third Law: action = −reaction
  • Ohm's Law: V = IR (Voltage = Current × Resistance)
  • Density = Mass / Volume
  • Acceleration due to gravity: g ≈ 9.8 m/s²; v = u + at, s = ut + ½at²
  • pH scale: pH < 7 acidic, pH = 7 neutral, pH > 7 basic
Topics Covered
  • Verbal Reasoning — analogy, classification, coding-decoding, blood relations, direction sense, series
  • Non-Verbal Reasoning — mirror/water images, paper folding, figure series, embedded figures
  • Numerical Aptitude — number system, simplification, average, ratio-proportion, percentage, profit-loss, time & work, time-speed-distance, simple/compound interest
  • Logical Venn diagrams & syllogisms
🧩 Quick Formulas
  • Average = Sum of observations / Number of observations
  • Percentage change = (Change / Original value) × 100
  • Profit % = (Profit / CP) × 100; Loss % = (Loss / CP) × 100
  • Time & Work: If A does work in x days, A's 1-day work = 1/x
  • If two work together: combined 1-day work = 1/x + 1/y
  • Direction sense: clockwise 90° turn right, anticlockwise 90° turn left
Topics Covered
  • General Science — Physics, Chemistry, Biology basics (Class 9–12 level for Tech entries)
  • General Knowledge — history, geography, polity, current affairs, sports
  • Mathematics — Class 10–12 level for Technical entries; basic arithmetic for GD
  • General Reasoning — coding, series, analogy, classification
  • Negative marking: 0.25 to 0.5 marks deducted per wrong answer (check current notification)
📐 Frequently Tested Formulas (Tech Entries)
  • Resistors in series: R = R₁+R₂+R₃; Parallel: 1/R = 1/R₁+1/R₂+1/R₃
  • Power: P = VI = I²R = V²/R
  • Heat: Q = mcΔT (mass × specific heat × temperature change)
  • Mole concept: Number of moles = Given mass / Molar mass
NDA & NA Written Exam
  • Paper 1 — Mathematics: 300 marks, 120 questions, 2.5 hours
  • Paper 2 — General Ability Test (English + GK): 600 marks, 150 questions, 2.5 hours
  • Negative marking: 1/3rd mark deducted per wrong answer
CDS Written Exam
  • IMA/INA/AFA: English (100), GK (100), Elementary Mathematics (100) — 2 hours each
  • OTA (Non-Technical): English (100) + GK (100) only — no Mathematics paper
  • Negative marking: 1/3rd mark deducted per wrong answer
AFCAT
  • General Awareness, Verbal Ability, Numerical Ability, Reasoning & Military Aptitude — 100 questions, 100 marks, 2 hours
  • Technical entries also attempt EKT (Engineering Knowledge Test) — 50 questions, 45 minutes
  • Negative marking: 1 mark deducted per wrong answer
CAPF (AC) Exam — UPSC
  • Paper 1 — General Ability & Intelligence: 250 marks, 2 hours, objective
  • Paper 2 — General Studies, Essay & Comprehension: 200 marks, 3 hours, descriptive
Agniveer (Army GD / Tech / Clerk, Navy SSR/MR, Vayu)
  • Computer-based online test; duration and question count vary by entry (typically 50–100 questions, 45–60 minutes)
  • Negative marking: 0.25–0.5 marks per wrong answer depending on the year's notification

Exact marks/duration are revised periodically by each recruiting body — always cross-check the current official notification before the exam.

Topics Covered
  • Computer fundamentals — hardware vs software, input/output devices, generations of computers
  • Operating systems basics — functions of an OS, common OS (Windows, Linux, Android)
  • MS Office basics — Word, Excel, PowerPoint common functions and shortcuts
  • Internet & Networking — LAN/WAN, browser, URL, email, basic cybersecurity terms
  • Number systems — binary, decimal, octal, hexadecimal conversions
  • Abbreviations — CPU, RAM, ROM, USB, HTTP, HTML, URL, ISP, Wi-Fi
💻 Quick Facts
  • 1 Byte = 8 Bits; 1 KB = 1024 Bytes; 1 MB = 1024 KB; 1 GB = 1024 MB
  • CPU = Central Processing Unit (the "brain" of the computer)
  • RAM is volatile (temporary) memory; ROM is non-volatile (permanent) memory
Firsts & Records
  • First Field Marshal of India — Sam Manekshaw and K.M. Cariappa
  • First Param Vir Chakra recipient — Major Somnath Sharma (1947–48 Kashmir Operations)
  • First woman fighter pilot of India — among the first batch commissioned in 2016 (Indian Air Force)
  • Highest battlefield in the world — Siachen Glacier, guarded by the Indian Army
  • First indigenous aircraft carrier — INS Vikrant (commissioned 2022)
Important Days (Quick Recall)
  • Kargil Vijay Diwas — 26 July; Infantry Day — 27 October; Vijay Diwas (1971 war) — 16 December
  • National Police Commemoration Day — 21 October
Headquarters & Apex Bodies
  • Ministry of Defence — New Delhi; Integrated Defence Staff HQ — New Delhi
  • National Defence College — New Delhi (for senior officer strategic training)
  • Institute for Defence Studies and Analyses (IDSA/MP-IDSA) — New Delhi, India's premier defence think-tank
🎯 Combined Preparation Strategy (All Categories)
  • Make a subject-wise weekly timetable — give Mathematics & Reasoning the most daily time since they're formula-heavy
  • Master NCERT Class 9–12 for Maths, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, History, Geography and Polity first
  • Maintain a single formula notebook — revise it every morning for 15–20 minutes
  • Read a newspaper or current-affairs digest daily for GK and defence news
  • Solve one previous year paper every 2–3 days under strict timed conditions
  • After every mock test, analyse mistakes and rewrite the missed formula/concept by hand
  • In the last month, switch fully to timed full-length mocks and revision — avoid new topics
  • Don't neglect physical fitness alongside written prep — most categories have PET/PFT before or after the written exam
BHARATDEFENCE

The Final Frontier

SSB Interview

The Services Selection Board (SSB) is a 5-day personality and intelligence assessment. It evaluates Officer-Like Qualities (OLQs) across three dimensions.

DAY 1

Screening Tests

OIR Test

Officer Intelligence Rating — Two sets of verbal and non-verbal reasoning questions. Scores candidates on intelligence rating IR1 to IR5. Used for initial screening.

PPDT

Picture Perception & Description Test — A hazy image is shown for 30 seconds. Candidates write a story (60 words), then discuss in groups. Tests perception, communication, and leadership.

Approximately 50–70% of candidates are screened out after Day 1.
DAY 2–3

Psychological Tests

TAT — Thematic Apperception Test

11 pictures + 1 blank slide. Write a story for each (4 minutes per picture). Stories reveal subconscious personality traits, values, and thinking patterns. Avoid negative themes — heroes must overcome challenges.

WAT — Word Association Test

60 words shown one at a time (15 seconds each). Write the first sentence that comes to mind. Tests spontaneity, positivity, and subconscious association patterns.

SRT — Situation Reaction Test

60 situations in 30 minutes. Respond with the first action that comes to mind. Reveals decision-making, common sense, social sensitivity, and initiative.

SD — Self Description

Write what your Parents, Teachers, Friends, and YOU think about you. Consistency with behavior observed during GTO and Interview is critical.

DAY 3–4

GTO Tasks

Group Discussion (GD)

2 rounds — Current Affairs & Abstract topic. Contribute meaningful points, encourage others to speak, avoid dominating or going silent.

Group Planning Exercise (GPE)

Analyze a crisis map, plan solutions individually, then discuss as a group. Tests analytical thinking and communication.

Progressive Group Task (PGT)

Group crosses 4 obstacles using limited materials (plank, rope, barrel). Cooperation, leadership, and initiative are observed.

Half Group Task (HGT)

Same as PGT but with a smaller group (5–6). Each person's contribution is more visible.

Individual Obstacles

10 obstacles, each with a point value (1–10). You have 3 minutes to score maximum points. Tests individual courage, speed, and technique.

Command Task

You lead 2–3 subordinates to solve a task. The GTO assigns subordinates based on observed performance. Pure leadership test.

Lecturette

3-minute talk on a topic chosen from 4 slips (1 minute preparation). Tests knowledge, communication, and confidence under pressure.

Final Group Task (FGT)

One final obstacle task for the whole group. Similar to PGT. Last chance to demonstrate OLQs.

DAY 4

Personal Interview

Conducted by an Interviewing Officer (IO). Duration: 45–60 minutes. Covers your life history form (PIQ), current affairs, defence GK, and personal values.

PIQ Form

Fill accurately and honestly. Every answer can be questioned in depth. Inconsistency between PIQ, SD, and spoken answers is a red flag.

Key Topics

Hometown & state, schooling, extracurriculars, family background, current affairs (national & defence), why the Armed Forces, future plans, strengths & weaknesses.

DAY 5

Conference & Medical

Conference

All three assessors (Psychologist, GTO, IO) along with the President review their assessments. Candidate is called in briefly. Board arrives at a consensus recommendation.

Medical Examination

Recommended candidates undergo a comprehensive medical at a Military Hospital. Specific standards exist for vision, height, weight, dental, and overall fitness. Medically unfit candidates can appeal.

The 15 Officer-Like Qualities (OLQs)

Effective IntelligenceReasoning Ability Organising AbilityPower of Expression Social AdaptabilityCooperation Sense of ResponsibilityInitiative Self ConfidenceSpeed of Decision Ability to InfluenceLiveliness DeterminationCourage Stamina

Preparation Guide

How to Crack SSB — Test-by-Test

Actionable strategies for every stage of the 5-day board

What is Tested

Officer Intelligence Rating (OIR) has two booklets — Verbal (analogies, synonyms, series, coding-decoding, sentence arrangement) and Non-Verbal (figure series, mirror images, pattern completion, odd-one-out, cube folding). Both are MCQ, strictly time-bound.

Target Score

Aim for IR-1 or IR-2 (top bands). Candidates below IR-4 rarely get screened in. Speed + accuracy both matter.

Practice Plan
  • Solve RS Aggarwal Verbal & Non-Verbal Reasoning — minimum 2 chapters daily
  • Practice figure series, mirror images, cube nets — these repeat in patterns
  • Timed practice: 40 questions in 17 minutes (simulate actual speed)
  • Coding-decoding and number series — high frequency topics
  • Apps: SSB OIR Practice, Reasoning Master
💡 Key Insight: OIR is not about knowledge — it's about processing speed. Practice daily for 30 days to see visible improvement.
What Happens

A hazy image is projected for 30 seconds. You then write a story (60 words, 4 minutes) covering: number of characters, age, mood, and what is happening. After writing, your group discusses and arrives at a common story in 15 minutes.

Story Formula
  • Background: What led to this situation
  • Action: What the hero is doing right now
  • Result: Positive outcome achieved
  • Hero must be active, resourceful, and successful
Discussion Strategy
  • Speak first or second — present your story clearly in 30 seconds
  • Listen actively — incorporate others' good points into the common story
  • Never argue or interrupt; disagree politely
  • Help the group converge — suggest a combined storyline
  • Maintain eye contact with group, not the assessor
✓ DO
  • Keep hero positive and proactive
  • Stick to 1 clear storyline
  • Encourage quiet members to speak
✗ DON'T
  • Write a negative ending
  • Dominate the group discussion
  • Change your story drastically to please others
Structure

11 pictures + 1 blank slide. Each picture shown for 30 seconds, then 4 minutes to write a story. The blank slide: write any story you want. Total time ~55 minutes.

Story Structure (4 Ws)
  • What led to the situation? (background)
  • What is happening now? (action)
  • What is the hero thinking/feeling?
  • What will happen next? (always positive outcome)
💡 Use one main character only — make them resourceful, decisive, and successful. The psychologist looks for pattern across all 11 stories.
Themes to Incorporate
  • Problem-solving and overcoming challenges
  • Leadership, teamwork, responsibility
  • Community service, national pride
  • Ethical decisions under pressure
  • Sports, adventure, technical achievement
✓ DO
  • Vary story themes across slides
  • Keep hero active, not passive
  • End every story positively
✗ DON'T
  • Write tragic/negative endings
  • Repeat same theme 11 times
  • Write vague or ambiguous stories
Structure

60 words shown one at a time on screen — 15 seconds each to write the first sentence that comes to mind. Total: 15 minutes. Words are ordinary (e.g. RIVER, MOTHER, FAILURE, JUNGLE, FRIEND).

What the Psychologist Sees

Patterns across all 60 responses reveal your subconscious attitude — optimism, social awareness, confidence, negativity, aggression. Responses must be sentences, not single words.

Response Framework
  • Write a complete sentence — Subject + Verb + Object
  • Keep it positive, action-oriented, and relevant
  • Relate to leadership, service, or achievement when natural
  • Don't overthink — first positive thought is best

Examples:

FAILURE → "Failure is a stepping stone to success if one learns from it."

JUNGLE → "He led his team through the jungle with confidence and calm."

ENEMY → "Soldiers guard the border against the enemy with full dedication."

💡 Practice daily: pick 10 random words, set a 15-second timer for each, write one sentence. Do this for 3 weeks.
Structure

60 situations in a booklet — 30 minutes to respond (30 seconds per situation). Write the first practical action you would take. Situations are everyday social, emergency, and moral dilemmas.

Sample Situations
  • "You see a road accident on your way to an important exam. You…"
  • "A junior colleague makes a serious error in a report. You…"
  • "You are lost in a forest with no phone signal. You…"
  • "Your friend asks you to lie to cover his mistake. You…"
Response Principles
  • Always take action — don't write "I will think about it"
  • Show initiative, social responsibility, and courage
  • Practical & common-sense response — not dramatic
  • Keep responses short (1–2 lines)
  • Don't abandon responsibility to help only yourself
✓ DO
  • Help others first if life is at risk
  • Show ethical decision-making
  • Write confidently and quickly
✗ DON'T
  • Write selfish responses
  • Leave situations unanswered
  • Over-explain — brevity wins
What You Write

In ~15 minutes, write what 5 people think about you:

  • Parents — What do they say about you?
  • Teachers — Your academic & personal traits
  • Friends — Social, fun, reliable aspects
  • You yourself — Honest self-assessment
  • What you want to improve — 1–2 real weaknesses
Critical Rules
  • Consistency is everything — SD must match your PIQ, GTO behavior, and Interview answers
  • Mention real, specific qualities — avoid generic words like "good, hardworking"
  • Weakness must be genuine but not fatal — e.g. "I get impatient when work is delayed" (not "I lose my temper")
  • Write in third person: "His friends say he is…"
💡 Write your SD draft at home and review it against your actual behavior. The IO will cross-check SD against your Interview responses on Day 4.
8 GTO Tasks Overview
  • GD (Group Discussion): 2 rounds — current affairs + abstract. Contribute quality points, don't dominate
  • GPE (Group Planning): Analyse a crisis scenario on a map, plan solutions, present to group
  • PGT (Progressive Group Task): 4 obstacles with group using limited equipment
  • HGT (Half Group Task): Same as PGT, smaller group — you're more visible
  • Individual Obstacles: 10 obstacles, 3 minutes, scored 1–10 per obstacle
  • Command Task: You lead, you choose your subordinates — pure leadership
  • Lecturette: 3-min talk from 4 topic options (1 min prep)
  • FGT (Final Group Task): Last group obstacle — consolidate OLQ display
Physical Preparation
  • Run 5 km daily — build endurance for outdoor tasks
  • Practice rope climbing, wall scaling, balance beam
  • Increase upper body strength — push-ups, pull-ups (3 sets daily)
  • Practice Lecturette topics: current affairs, defence, environment, sports, technology
Leadership Tips
  • Give clear, confident instructions in Command Task
  • In PGT/HGT — contribute ideas AND help execute, not just order
  • Encourage silent teammates verbally during GD
  • GTO is watching — be consistent even when you think you're not observed
What to Prepare
  • PIQ Form — Fill every field carefully; IO digs deep into it
  • About yourself: hometown, school, family, hobbies, achievements
  • Why Armed Forces? — Specific, genuine answer (not "to serve the nation" only)
  • Current affairs: last 6 months — national, defence, international
  • Defence GK: Indian Army structure, recent ops, major weapons, DRDO, ISRO
  • Strengths & weaknesses — consistent with SD
During the Interview
  • Enter confidently — firm handshake, sit straight
  • Maintain steady eye contact — don't look away when answering
  • If you don't know something — say "Sir, I'm not aware of this, but I'll find out." Never bluff
  • Keep answers crisp and direct — avoid rambling
  • Show genuine enthusiasm for the service you're applying for
💡 Stress Interview: Some IOs deliberately challenge or contradict you. Stay calm, smile, and hold your ground politely. Changing answers under pressure = failure signal.
Week 1
Foundation
  • OIR: Verbal reasoning — analogies, series, coding (RS Aggarwal)
  • OIR: Non-verbal — figure series, mirror images, cube nets
  • Start daily 5 km run
  • Read one national newspaper daily
  • Write PIQ form draft — get family to review
Week 2
Psychology Tests
  • TAT: Write 2 stories daily from hazy images (find online)
  • WAT: 20 words/day — 15-second timer per word
  • SRT: 10 situations/day — write first reaction
  • SD: Draft all 5 perspectives, review for consistency
  • Physical: add push-ups 50/day, pull-ups 10/day
Week 3
GTO & Interview
  • Prepare 10 Lecturette topics — practice 3-min talks
  • Group Discussion: join online SSB prep groups
  • Interview: prepare hometown, family, school, why forces
  • Defence GK: Indian Army structure, major operations, weapons
  • Physical: rope climbing, obstacle practice if possible
Week 4
Full Simulation
  • Full mock psychology session: TAT(11) + WAT(60) + SRT(60) timed
  • Mock personal interview with a friend or mentor
  • Review current affairs: last 6 months defence & national
  • Pack for board: documents, fitness gear, formal clothes
  • Sleep 8 hrs, eat clean — arrive fresh and confident
BHARATDEFENCE

SSB Practice Toolkit

PPDT, TAT, WAT, SRT & GTO Practice Hub

A massively expanded practice zone for SSB aspirants — 200+ OIR questions, 12 PPDT sets, 8 TAT story-blocks, 60 WAT words, 60 SRT situations, 18 GTO drills (including dedicated Snake Race & Obstacle Race) and 5 SD/PI rehearsals. Use these timed drills exactly like the board environment: write fast, think positive, stay practical, and review your responses for Officer Like Qualities.

How to Use

  • Attempt each module under actual SSB timing.
  • Keep responses practical, optimistic, and action-oriented.
  • Focus on initiative, teamwork, courage, and social effectiveness.
  • Review answers after every set and rewrite weak ones.

Self-Check Lens

  • Does the hero take responsibility instead of waiting?
  • Is the response realistic and socially mature?
  • Did you show planning, empathy, and calmness under pressure?
  • Is the conclusion positive without sounding filmi or fake?

Minimum Weekly Volume

  • PPDT: 6 stories + 6 spoken narrations (use Sets 1-12)
  • TAT: 24 stories across the week (Sets 1-8)
  • WAT: All 60 words timed at 15 seconds each
  • SRT: 60 situations spread across 2 sittings
  • GTO: 2 GDs + 1 Lecturette + 1 Planning + Snake/Obstacle Race drill

Peer Practice

  • Discuss PPDT and GTO themes with 3-5 friends.
  • Record your lecturette for voice clarity and confidence.
  • Ask a friend to cross-question your personal interview answers.
  • Keep a notebook of repeated mistakes and better rewrites.
OIR Verbal & Non-Verbal Screening Practice Psychology Sets GTO Drills Lecturette Topics Interview & Self Description

OIR Verbal Reasoning Sets

Practice SSB Screening Stage 1: Officer Intelligence Rating verbal questions. Each set has 10 questions to be attempted in 7 minutes. Tap any set to start timed practice.

OIR VERBAL
10 sets · 7 min each 10 Qs per set · 100 Qs total Vocabulary & Logic IR-1 to IR-5 scoring
Verbal Set 1

Synonyms, Antonyms & Analogies

10 questions covering word meaning, opposites, and word-pair relationships.

Verbal Set 2

Number Series & Coding-Decoding

10 questions on number patterns, letter coding, and sequence completion.

Verbal Set 3

Classification & Odd One Out

10 questions: find the item that does not belong in a group of words or concepts.

Verbal Set 4

Statement Conclusions & Syllogisms

10 logical reasoning questions — determine which conclusions follow from given statements.

Verbal Set 5

Sentence Completion & Jumbled Words

10 questions on filling blanks with the most appropriate word and rearranging letters.

Verbal Set 6

Direction & Blood Relations

10 questions on directional logic and family relationship problems.

Verbal Set 7

Alphabet & Letter Series

10 questions identifying letter patterns and missing alphabets in sequences.

Verbal Set 8

Ranking & Order Arrangement

10 questions on ranking, ordering, and seating arrangement puzzles.

Verbal Set 9

Cause & Effect / Course of Action

10 questions assessing logical reasoning for decision-making scenarios.

Verbal Set 10

Vocabulary Master Test

10 high-difficulty vocabulary and reasoning questions — final mastery check.

OIR Non-Verbal Reasoning Sets

Practice figure series, mirror images, cube-folding, and spatial reasoning. Each set has 10 questions in 7 minutes. These directly test spatial and abstract intelligence scored at SSB Stage 1.

OIR NON-VERBAL
10 sets · 7 min each 10 Qs per set · 100 Qs total Figures & Patterns Spatial Intelligence
Non-Verbal Set 1

Figure Series Completion

10 questions — identify the next figure in a sequence based on shape, size, rotation, or shading patterns.

Non-Verbal Set 2

Mirror Images & Water Images

10 questions — select the correct mirror/water image of a given figure from four options.

Non-Verbal Set 3

Cube Nets & 3D Folding

10 questions — identify which 3D shape is formed when a given 2D net is folded.

Non-Verbal Set 4

Embedded Figures & Hidden Shapes

10 questions — find a small figure hidden within a larger complex figure.

Non-Verbal Set 5

Matrices & Pattern Completion

10 questions — complete a 3x3 matrix of figures by identifying the rule applied row by row.

Non-Verbal Set 6

Paper Folding & Cutting

10 questions — visualise the result when paper is folded and cut at specific angles.

Non-Verbal Set 7

Analogy of Figures

10 questions — find the figure that completes the analogy pair like figure-X is to figure-Y.

Non-Verbal Set 8

Dot Situation & Rule Detection

10 questions — identify the figure where a dot can be placed under specific conditions.

Non-Verbal Set 9

Counting of Figures

10 questions — count triangles, squares, rectangles, and complex figures inside a design.

Non-Verbal Set 10

Mixed Spatial Master Test

10 high-difficulty mixed spatial reasoning questions — combined final challenge.

PPDT Practice Sets

Look at the situation mentally for 30 seconds, note characters-age-mood, then write a 60-word story in 4 minutes and narrate it in under 1 minute.

PPDT
12 sets · 30 sec perception 4 min story 1 min narration Group discussion follow-up
Set 1

Flood Relief Volunteer

A 20-year-old NCC cadet notices villagers stranded after heavy rain near a broken culvert.

  • Decide hero age, mood, and immediate objective.
  • Show organization of help, safety, and communication.
  • End with measurable positive result.
Set 2

Highway Accident Witness

A college student sees a bike skid near a crowded crossing during evening traffic.

  • Prioritize safety, ambulance call, and crowd control.
  • Avoid drama; show calm, practical action.
  • Give the story a timely outcome.
Set 3

Village Study Group Leader

A senior student sees juniors performing poorly before board exams in a remote village school.

  • Create a realistic initiative with limited resources.
  • Use teamwork and community support.
  • Show improvement through disciplined effort.
Set 4

Trekking Route Problem

A youth group leader finds that their route is blocked by a fallen tree and one member is panicking.

  • Display leadership, reassurance, and alternate planning.
  • Keep all team members involved.
  • Conclude with safe completion of mission.
Set 5

Fire in Local Market

A young man notices smoke rising from a shop in a busy local market during peak hours.

  • Alert fire brigade quickly and evacuate nearby shops.
  • Form a chain to pass water buckets till help arrives.
  • Help the elderly and children clear the area first.
Set 6

Stranded Bus Passengers

A college boy is in a bus that breaks down on a deserted highway at night with elderly passengers.

  • Reassure passengers and check for medical emergencies.
  • Coordinate signalling for help and ration available resources.
  • Show calm leadership through the cold/dark night.
Set 7

Sports Team Captain

A girl is appointed captain of an underperforming college sports team just before a tournament.

  • Diagnose why the team is losing morale.
  • Build a daily training and team-bonding routine.
  • End with visible improvement in the next match.
Set 8

Hostel Hygiene Drive

A hostel prefect finds growing illness due to unclean surroundings in the hostel premises.

  • Mobilise hostel mates into a cleaning team.
  • Involve warden and contractor with a written plan.
  • Show measurable improvement in health/conditions.
Set 9

Lost Child at Railway Station

A youngster spots a lost child crying near the platform exit during evening rush hours.

  • Calm the child first and gather basic information.
  • Approach RPF/announcer for help, not strangers.
  • Stay with the child till parents are reunited.
Set 10

Cyber Fraud Awareness

A college volunteer learns that several elders in his colony lost money to a fake call scam.

  • Organise a quick awareness session in the colony park.
  • Help victims file complaints at the cyber cell.
  • Show a follow-up that prevents the next attempt.
Set 11

Pollution in Local Pond

A young engineer sees a community pond getting polluted by nearby workshops just before a festival.

  • Document the issue and involve municipal officials.
  • Mobilise youth volunteers for a clean-up drive.
  • End with a sustainable plan to keep it clean.
Set 12

College Election Crisis

A senior student sees rising aggression between two campaigning groups during college elections.

  • Mediate calmly between both group leaders.
  • Suggest a code of conduct supervised by faculty.
  • Restore peaceful elections with fair participation.

TAT Practice Set

Attempt these as 12 consecutive slides: 11 pictured situations and 1 blank slide. Keep your main character close to your own age and profile.

TAT
8 sets · 4 min per story 22+ picture themes 2 blank stories Action + outcome focus
Slides 1–4
  • A cadet studies late because his unit test performance has dropped.
  • A young woman coordinates a blood donation camp in her college.
  • A boy notices smoke from a workshop near a residential lane.
  • A sports team loses badly before the zonal finals.
Slides 5–8
  • A trainee engineer is sent to a village to restore a water pump.
  • A student mentor finds a friend skipping classes due to family stress.
  • A youth expedition reaches a checkpoint later than expected.
  • A group wants to clean a polluted pond before a local festival.
Slides 9–11
  • A football captain must motivate his team after their star player is injured.
  • A hostel prefect handles rising conflict between two room groups.
  • A student volunteers to help a primary school recover after storm damage.
Blank Slide

Your Life Goal Story

Write a realistic story from your own ambition: joining the armed forces, preparing through discipline, facing setbacks, improving, and finally contributing to society in uniform.

Set 5 — Slides 1–4
  • A young farmer adopts modern irrigation to save his village from drought.
  • A girl convinces her family to let her sister continue higher studies.
  • A college NCC senior trains juniors before the Republic Day parade.
  • An office intern notices a safety hazard near a construction site.
Set 6 — Slides 5–8
  • A teacher organises an evening tuition centre for slum children.
  • A youth notices an old man collapse at a bus stand during peak heat.
  • A trekking leader spots a member falling behind on a steep climb.
  • A college fest organiser faces a last-minute power failure on stage.
Set 7 — Slides 9–11
  • A medical student helps run a rural health awareness camp.
  • A startup founder must lead his team through a sudden setback.
  • A youth volunteer trains villagers in basic disaster response.
Blank Slide — Service Vision

Your Service Vision Story

Imagine yourself five years into your armed forces career. Write a clear story showing: how you joined, one operational or training challenge you handled with your team, the OLQs you used, and what your unit achieved because of your leadership.

WAT Practice Bank

Use a 15-second timer for each word. Write the first meaningful sentence that reflects positivity, responsibility, and mental clarity.

WAT
60 words · 15 sec each No overthinking Simple natural sentences OLQ-oriented association
DutyWrite a sentence showing responsibility.
FearShow courage with awareness, not recklessness.
TeamReflect cooperation and trust.
FailureShow learning and comeback.
MotherKeep it respectful and warm.
LeaderShow service and initiative.
MoneyReflect discipline and ethics.
RiskBalance courage with judgement.
VillageThink development and community.
FriendShow loyalty and guidance.
NightKeep the image purposeful, not gloomy.
DisciplineLink it with daily habits.
ProblemFocus on solution mindset.
InjuryShow calm first aid and recovery.
TrainThink movement, punctuality, planning.
ExamLink preparation with confidence.
FarmerReflect dignity and support.
UniformShow honour and service.
WeaknessConvert it into improvement.
TimeDemonstrate value and discipline.
WomanUse respect, capability, leadership.
MountainShow endurance and planning.
ResponsibilityConnect action with accountability.
LossShow maturity and resilience.
CourageShow calm strength in tough moments.
HonestyLink truth with daily conduct.
AngerShow controlled reaction, not outburst.
SacrificeReflect higher purpose without drama.
OfficerUse service mindset and humility.
BorderReflect alertness and patriotism.
NationShow pride through contribution.
StrengthCombine physical and mental aspects.
DefeatShow recovery and lessons learnt.
VictoryStay humble and credit the team.
SoldierReflect duty, sacrifice, discipline.
FamilyShow warmth and shared responsibility.
KnowledgeLink it with action and growth.
SpeedShow focus, not recklessness.
TruthReflect integrity in tough situations.
HelpShow timely and practical action.
HopeLink it with effort, not waiting.
CrowdShow calm leadership in a group.
DangerShow alertness and quick action.
SchoolReflect foundation and growth.
CaptainLead by example, not by orders.
MistakeOwn it, learn fast, move on.
HealthLink daily habits with performance.
GoalShow planning and steady effort.
CountryExpress service-based patriotism.
PunctualityShow its value in real life.
PatienceCombine it with steady action.
TrustLink it with consistent conduct.
ConflictResolve it calmly and fairly.
PressureShow composure under stress.
LonelinessConvert it into focus and self-work.
BattleReflect resolve, not aggression.
SweatLink effort with progress.
DrillShow discipline and routine.
ServiceReflect duty above self.
DreamTie it to disciplined action.

SRT Practice Situations

Respond in one quick action line. Think: what will I do first, whom will I help, and how will I solve it practically?

SRT
60 situations · 30 sec each Immediate action Practical + humane No preaching
1.You are late for an important exam and see an injured cyclist on the road.
2.Your teammate panics just before a public presentation.
3.Two friends begin fighting in the hostel corridor.
4.Your train is delayed and you must reach SSB reporting by morning.
5.You find that your group has lost its route map during a trek.
6.A child is crying alone in a crowded fair.
7.Your father wants you to join a family business, but you want the armed forces.
8.A classroom fan sparks and students start rushing toward the door.
9.You are made captain of a team that does not cooperate with you.
10.Your close friend is cheating in an exam and asks you to stay silent.
11.Your college event budget suddenly gets reduced by half.
12.While travelling, you notice a suspicious unattended bag at a bus stand.
13.A village elder rejects your plan for a cleanliness drive.
14.You fail the written exam once after months of preparation.
15.Your junior wants to quit sports after repeated defeats.
16.Your wallet is stolen in another city and you have no cash for the night.
17.A senior at SSB tries to demoralise you the night before psychology tests.
18.You see a co-passenger fall sick on a long-distance train.
19.Your group leader at GTO gives a wrong direction during a task.
20.You witness ragging of a junior outside the college canteen.
21.Your bike breaks down in a forest area as the sun sets.
22.Your mother is unwell and you have an interview the same morning.
23.Your team is stuck on a planning exercise without consensus.
24.A friend is being bullied online and the situation is worsening.
25.You spot a small fire in your school chemistry lab during a practical.
26.An unknown person asks for your hostel address late at night.
27.Your laptop crashes the day before your project submission.
28.A drunk man is harassing a girl at a bus stop and people are watching silently.
29.You overhear plans for a strike that may turn violent in your campus.
30.Your sister is being pressurised into an early marriage by relatives.
31.Your scholarship is suddenly cancelled due to a paperwork error.
32.You are leading a trek and the weather suddenly turns dangerous.
33.Your jeep is stuck in mud on a remote road at night.
34.A road accident victim needs blood and you are O+.
35.You see a stranger trying to break into a parked car near your home.
36.Your team loses a match because of your own missed catch / mistake.
37.Your group at SSB is short on time for a planning task.
38.You discover your roommate is using drugs during exam season.
39.A senior at GTO tries to dominate the entire group discussion.
40.Your phone falls into a river during a trek and you are off-route.
41.You are told mid-flight that the connecting train was cancelled.
42.Your school principal asks you to lead a sudden cultural function.
43.A poor family in your neighbourhood cannot afford their child's school fees.
44.You are asked to give a speech on Independence Day with a few hours' notice.
45.A senior soldier in your NCC unit acts rudely toward a junior cadet.
46.Your father loses his job and family income drops sharply.
47.Your group at GTO is given a heavier load than allowed by the rules.
48.You see a small child running across a busy road, unaware of traffic.
49.An old lady faints inside a crowded local train.
50.You are asked to handle a stranger's medical emergency in a remote bus stop.
51.Your name is wrongly added to a college disciplinary list.
52.Two teammates blame each other publicly after losing a match.
53.You see a stray dog injured on a busy market street.
54.A loved one is hospitalised on the day of your written test.
55.You are stopped by police for an offence you did not commit.
56.You miss your alarm on the day of SSB conference.
57.Your group is shouting and getting personal during a heated GD.
58.An unknown caller threatens your family for past financial dealings.
59.Your hostel mess workers go on a sudden strike during exams.
60.You are asked to swim across a fast river to help a child stranded on an island.

GTO Practice Themes

Use these topics for group discussion, lecturette, planning exercise, command task briefing, and obstacle rehearsal with friends.

GTO
18 GTO drills GD · Lecturette · PE · CT Snake Race · Obstacle Race FGT · HGT · Individual Obstacles
GD 1Is social media helping or weakening youth leadership?
GD 2Should short-service opportunities be expanded for educated youth?
Lecturette 1Disaster response and the role of trained citizens.
Lecturette 2Women in combat support and leadership roles.
Planning Exercise 1Bus breakdown, injured passenger, exam reporting deadline, limited cash.
Command Task 1Get a medicine box across a mock obstacle using rope, plank, and helpers.
PGT / HGT MindsetTalk aloud, involve team, respect helping material rules.
Snake RaceCreate chants, motivate the slowest member, finish together.
Individual ObstaclesRope climb, zig-zag balance, jump confidence, time judgement.
Obstacle Race (Group)Group obstacle relay — pass the snake/log over multiple obstacles, finish as one unit.
GD 3Is the Agnipath scheme good for India's defence preparedness?
Lecturette 3Cybersecurity threats and a youth-led response.
Planning Exercise 2Train accident: injured passengers, blocked road, distant hospital, limited daylight.
Command Task 2Cross a 'crocodile river' carrying a wounded soldier — only 2 helpers, 1 plank, 1 rope.
Full Group Task (FGT)Whole group crosses a 12-foot ditch with rope, plank and barrel — no member left behind.
Half Group Task (HGT)Half-group attempts a tricky PGT-style task — same rules, smaller team, more individual exposure.
Lecturette 4Role of youth in disaster management — 3 min preparation, 3 min delivery.
Group Obstacle Confidence DrillFinal mock combining Snake Race, Obstacle Race and Individual Obstacles back-to-back.

Self Description & Interview Builder

Build consistency between your psychology tests, GTO behaviour, and personal interview answers.

PI / SD
5 SD + PI sets PIQ deep dive Current affairs & defence Consistency check
Self Description

5 SD Paragraphs

  • Parents think I am...
  • Teachers think I am...
  • Friends think I am...
  • I think I am...
  • I want to improve in...
Interview Rehearsal 1

Why-You Questions

  • Why do you want to join the armed forces?
  • What have you done after your last failure?
  • What responsibilities do you handle at home?
  • Who is your role model and why?
  • What are three weaknesses you are actively improving?
Interview Rehearsal 2

PIQ Deep Dive

  • Walk me through your school and college life choices.
  • Which subject did you find toughest and how did you cope?
  • Tell me about one event you organised and what went wrong.
  • Describe one decision in life you took against advice — outcome?
  • What are your hobbies and what have you achieved in them?
Interview Rehearsal 3

Current Affairs & Defence

  • What is your view on the Agnipath scheme?
  • Name one recent operation by the Indian Armed Forces and what you learnt.
  • What are India's biggest external security threats today?
  • What is the role of CDS (Chief of Defence Staff)?
  • Where do you see yourself in the forces 10 years from now?
Self Description 2

Improvement & Vision

  • One habit I have improved in the last 6 months and how.
  • One responsibility I have taken at home or college recently.
  • One person whose feedback changed how I behave.
  • One quality I want my future juniors to remember me for.
  • One concrete goal for the next 90 days and my plan for it.
Practice Rule: Do not memorize fancy model answers. The selection board checks your natural thought process. Use this toolkit to build speed, clarity, positivity, and grounded leadership traits — not artificial scripting.
Best use: 45-60 min daily Rewrite weak responses next day Practice narration aloud Stay natural, practical, positive

3-Day Fast Drill

  • Day 1: PPDT x 2 + WAT 30 words
  • Day 2: TAT 6 stories + SRT 15 situations
  • Day 3: GD 2 topics + lecturette 2 topics + SD draft

Weekly Full Mock

  • PPDT one full cycle with narration
  • TAT 12 stories in sequence
  • WAT 60 words + SRT 30 situations timed
  • One GTO discussion and one lecturette recording

What to Improve Every Week

  • Reduce negative framing
  • Use shorter, clearer sentences
  • Show initiative without dominating everyone
  • Make conclusions specific and outcome-based
BHARATDEFENCE

Test Your Knowledge

Mock Test

A timed quiz covering NDA/CDS level General Knowledge, Mathematics, and English. Your score is saved to your dashboard.

Select one or more subjects — tap the card or the checkbox.

General Knowledge

Defence, History, Polity, Geography

Current Affairs

National & International Events

Mathematics

Arithmetic, Algebra, Geometry

English

Grammar, Vocabulary, Comprehension

Reasoning

Verbal & Non-Verbal Reasoning

General Science

Physics, Chemistry, Biology

Defence Awareness

Ranks, Equipment, Wars, Organisations
