BHARATDEFENCE

Know Your Forces

Armed Forces Equipment

Tanks, ships, aircraft, missiles & small arms in service with the Indian Armed Forces — essential GK for SSB interviews, Lecturettes & written exams.

T-90 Bhishma
Russia/India

Main Battle Tank — backbone of Army's armoured strike corps

Crew: 3  |  Main Gun: 125mm smoothbore  |  Speed: ~60 km/h  |  Built under license by HVF Avadi.

Arjun Mk1A
India

Indigenous Main Battle Tank — DRDO designed

Crew: 4  |  Main Gun: 120mm rifled  |  Armour: Kanchan composite  |  Over 70 upgrades vs Mk1.

T-72 Ajeya
Russia/India

Legacy Main Battle Tank, being phased out gradually

Crew: 3  |  Main Gun: 125mm  |  In service since the 1980s, locally upgraded.

BMP-2 Sarath
Russia/India

Infantry Combat Vehicle — amphibious, troop-carrying

Crew: 3 + 7 troops  |  Armament: 30mm autocannon + ATGM launcher.

WhAP 8x8
India

Wheeled Armoured Platform — Tata Advanced Systems

Role: Troop transport / reconnaissance  |  Amphibious, modular weapon stations.

K9 Vajra-T
S. Korea/India

155mm/52cal self-propelled howitzer

Range: ~40 km  |  Built by L&T under license, deployed along western front.

Dhanush
India

155mm/45cal towed howitzer, "desi Bofors"

Range: ~38 km  |  Indigenously upgraded variant of the Bofors FH-77B.

Pinaka MBRL
India

Multi-Barrel Rocket Launcher

Range: 38–75 km (Mk variants)  |  12 rockets per battery, salvo in ~44 seconds.

M777 Ultra-Light Howitzer
USA

Towed, helicopter-portable 155mm howitzer

Range: ~25–30 km  |  Deployed in high-altitude sectors like Ladakh.

Akash SAM
India

Medium-range surface-to-air missile system

Range: ~25–30 km  |  Protects vulnerable air assets & ground formations.

S-400 Triumf
Russia

Long-range surface-to-air missile system

Range: up to 400 km  |  Inducted as "Sudarshan Chakra"; can track multiple aerial threats.

BrahMos
India-Russia

Supersonic cruise missile, land/sea/air launched

Speed: Mach ~2.8–3  |  Range: ~290–450 km depending on variant.

AK-203
India-Russia

Standard infantry assault rifle, replacing INSAS

Calibre: 7.62x39mm  |  Manufactured at Korwa, Amethi.

SIG716 / SIG Sauer
USA

Assault rifle for frontline infantry battalions

Calibre: 7.62x51mm  |  Fast-track procurement for forward-deployed units.

INSAS Rifle
India

Legacy service rifle, gradually being phased out

Calibre: 5.56x45mm  |  In service since the early 1990s.

Nag ATGM
India

Third-generation, fire-and-forget anti-tank guided missile

Range: ~4 km  |  Mounted on the NAMICA carrier vehicle.

INS Vikrant
India

Indigenous Aircraft Carrier (IAC-1)

Displacement: ~45,000 tonnes  |  Carries MiG-29K & Tejas Navy fighters; built at Cochin Shipyard.

INS Vikramaditya
Russia

STOBAR Aircraft Carrier, ex-Admiral Gorshkov

Displacement: ~45,000 tonnes  |  Flagship of the Western Fleet.

Visakhapatnam-class
India

Guided-missile destroyer (Project 15B)

Stealth design; armed with BrahMos & Barak-8 missiles.

Kolkata-class
India

Guided-missile destroyer (Project 15A)

Among the largest destroyers built indigenously to date.

Shivalik-class
India

Stealth multi-role frigate (Project 17)

First major stealth-design warships built in India.

Talwar-class
Russia

Guided-missile frigate

Based on the Krivak III design, armed with BrahMos.

Kalvari-class (Scorpene)
France/India

Diesel-electric attack submarine

Built at Mazagon Dock under technology transfer from Naval Group.

Arihant-class
India

Nuclear-powered ballistic missile submarine (SSBN)

Completes India's nuclear triad alongside land & air-based deterrents.

Sindhughosh-class (Kilo)
Russia

Diesel-electric attack submarine

In service since the late 1980s, periodically upgraded.

Kamorta-class
India

Anti-submarine warfare corvette

High degree of indigenous content, low radiated noise design.

Saryu-class
India

Offshore Patrol Vessel

Used for EEZ surveillance and anti-piracy patrols.

MiG-29K
Russia

Carrier-borne multirole fighter

Primary fighter operated off INS Vikramaditya & Vikrant.

Tejas Navy
India

Indigenous carrier-capable light fighter (under induction)

Twin-seat & single-seat variants tested for carrier ops.

P-8I Poseidon
USA

Long-range maritime patrol & anti-submarine aircraft

Based on the Boeing 737, equipped with sonobuoys & Harpoon missiles.

MH-60R Seahawk
USA

Multi-role naval helicopter

Anti-submarine & anti-surface warfare from frigates/destroyers.

Su-30MKI
Russia/India

Air superiority / multirole fighter, IAF's mainstay

Built under license by HAL; carries BrahMos-A & Astra missiles.

Rafale
France

4.5-generation multirole fighter

Equipped with Meteor BVR & SCALP cruise missiles; operates from Ambala & Hasimara.

Tejas Mk1A
India

Indigenous light combat aircraft

AESA radar & modern EW suite; progressively replacing MiG-21.

Mirage 2000
France

Multirole fighter, upgraded to Mirage 2000-I/TI standard

Used in the 1999 Kargil War & the 2019 Balakot strike.

MiG-29
Russia

Air superiority fighter (upgraded to UPG standard)

Known as "Baaz"; modernised avionics & in-flight refuelling capability.

C-17 Globemaster III
USA

Heavy strategic transport aircraft

Used for troop, tank & disaster-relief airlift; supports high-altitude ops.

C-130J Super Hercules
USA

Tactical transport for special-forces insertion

Capable of short/unprepared-runway operations.

IL-78 Mid-Air Refueller
Russia

Air-to-air refuelling tanker

Extends combat range of fighter fleets during deep strike missions.

Apache AH-64E
USA

Attack helicopter

Armed with Hellfire missiles & a 30mm chain gun.

Chinook CH-47F
USA

Heavy-lift transport helicopter

Moves howitzers & supplies to high-altitude border posts.

Dhruv ALH
India

Advanced Light Helicopter — utility & armed variants

Designed by HAL; used by Army, Navy, Air Force & Coast Guard.

Prachand LCH
India

Indigenous Light Combat Helicopter

Built for high-altitude attack roles; first flown operationally in Ladakh.

Netra AEW&C
India

Airborne Early Warning & Control aircraft

Indigenous radar system mounted on an Embraer airframe.

Astra Missile
India

Beyond Visual Range air-to-air missile

Range: ~110 km (Mk1)  |  Integrated on Su-30MKI & Tejas.

S-400 Triumf
Russia

Long-range air defence missile system

Shared deployment doctrine with the Army's air defence units.

Vikram-class OPV
India

Offshore Patrol Vessel

Used for EEZ patrol, anti-smuggling & search-and-rescue ops.

Dornier Do-228
Germany/India

Maritime surveillance aircraft

Built by HAL; used for coastal & EEZ surveillance.

ALH Dhruv
India

Search-and-rescue / surveillance helicopter

Operates from Coast Guard ships & coastal air stations.

Mine Protected Vehicles
India

Armoured vehicle for anti-Naxal & border ops

Used by CRPF in mine/IED-prone areas of central India.

INSAS / AK-47 Rifles
India

Standard service rifles for border guarding forces

Used by BSF & ITBP for static post and patrol duties.

Border Surveillance UAVs
India

Unmanned aerial systems for round-the-clock border watch

Deployed along the India-Pakistan & India-Bangladesh borders by BSF.

BHARATDEFENCE

Chain of Command

Ranks & Hierarchy

Officer and non-officer rank structures of the Army, Navy & Air Force, with the role and authority attached to each rank — frequently asked in SSB interviews and GK papers.

The Army has three broad tiers: Commissioned Officers (lead and command), Junior Commissioned Officers or JCOs (bridge officers and soldiers), and Other Ranks (the main fighting strength).

Field Marshal
Officer

Five-star ceremonial rank, the highest in the Army

Conferred only for exceptional wartime service; held by just two officers in Indian history. Never retires from service.

General
Officer

Chief of Army Staff — overall head of the Indian Army

Four-star rank. Commands the entire Army and reports to the Ministry of Defence.

Lieutenant General
Officer

Commands a Corps

Oversees several divisions in a theatre or sector; also holds Army Commander and senior staff appointments.

Major General
Officer

Commands a Division

Responsible for a major combat formation of roughly 15,000–20,000 troops.

Brigadier
Officer

Commands a Brigade

Leads a formation of several battalions; also holds senior staff appointments at Corps/Command level.

Colonel
Officer

Commands a Regiment or large unit

Often the Commanding Officer of a battalion-sized unit, or a senior staff officer.

Lieutenant Colonel
Officer

Second-in-Command or Commanding Officer of a battalion

Manages day-to-day operations and discipline of the unit.

Major
Officer

Company Commander

Leads a company of roughly 100–120 soldiers; also takes on staff appointments.

Captain
Officer

Commands a sub-unit within a company

Holds key tactical leadership and administrative duties at sub-unit level.

Lieutenant
Officer

Platoon Commander

The first command appointment given to a newly commissioned officer, leading roughly 30 soldiers.

Subedar Major
JCO

Senior-most JCO in a battalion

Principal advisor to the Commanding Officer on matters concerning the soldiers; bridges officers and other ranks.

Subedar
JCO

Company-level administration and training

Assists the Company Commander in managing troops, discipline and welfare.

Naib Subedar
JCO

Platoon-level administration

Supervises platoon administration and supports the Subedar in company-level duties.

Havildar
NCO

Section Commander

Leads a section of roughly 8–10 soldiers; equivalent of a sergeant.

Naik
NCO

Second-in-Command of a section

Assists the Havildar and steps in to lead the section when required.

Lance Naik
Soldier

Senior soldier with limited supervisory duties

First promotion rank above Sepoy, given added responsibility within the section.

Sepoy
Soldier

Basic soldier rank

Entry-level rank after training; forms the core fighting strength of the Army.

The Navy's tiers are Commissioned Officers (lead and command ships/formations) and Sailors (specialist ratings, the Navy's equivalent of JCOs and NCOs).

Admiral
Officer

Chief of Naval Staff — overall head of the Indian Navy

Four-star rank; commands the entire Navy and reports to the Ministry of Defence.

Vice Admiral
Officer

Commands a Naval Command

Heads one of the Navy's regional commands — Western, Eastern or Southern.

Rear Admiral
Officer

Commands a Fleet or major shore establishment

Leads a group of warships operating together, or a major training/logistics base.

Commodore
Officer

Senior staff officer or flotilla commander

Commands a flotilla of ships or holds a senior staff appointment at Command headquarters.

Captain
Officer

Commands a major warship or shore establishment

Typically commands destroyers, frigates or large naval bases.

Commander
Officer

Commands a smaller warship, or heads a department aboard a larger one

Holds key departmental responsibility — navigation, weapons or engineering.

Lieutenant Commander
Officer

Divisional Officer aboard ship

Manages a division of sailors and a specific departmental function on board.

Lieutenant
Officer

Watchkeeping Officer

Holds bridge watchkeeping duties and junior departmental responsibilities.

Sub Lieutenant
Officer

First commissioned rank after training

Entry rank for newly commissioned naval officers, under supervision of senior officers.

Master Chief Petty Officer 1st Class
Sailor

Senior-most sailor rank, equivalent to Subedar Major

Principal advisor to the ship's Captain on matters concerning sailors.

Master Chief Petty Officer 2nd Class
Sailor

Senior technical or administrative sailor

Heads a specialist department such as engineering or weapons systems.

Chief Petty Officer
Sailor

Supervises a team of Petty Officers and Seamen

Holds supervisory and training responsibilities within a department.

Petty Officer
Sailor

Leads a small specialist team

Carries out technical duties and supervises a handful of junior sailors.

Leading Seaman
Sailor

Experienced rating with junior supervisory duties

First promotion rank above Seaman, with some charge of duties.

Seaman
Sailor

Entry-level sailor rank

Basic rank after recruit training; the foundation of the Navy's manpower.

The Air Force's tiers are Commissioned Officers (lead flying and ground operations) and Non-Commissioned Ranks (technical and support backbone of the Force).

Marshal of the IAF
Officer

Five-star ceremonial rank, the highest in the Air Force

Conferred only once in Indian history, for exceptional service. Never retires from service.

Air Chief Marshal
Officer

Chief of Air Staff — overall head of the Indian Air Force

Four-star rank; commands the entire Air Force and reports to the Ministry of Defence.

Air Marshal
Officer

Commands an operational Command

Heads one of the IAF's regional commands, overseeing multiple air bases.

Air Vice Marshal
Officer

Commands a Group within a Command

Oversees several air force stations grouped under one operational area.

Air Commodore
Officer

Commands a major Air Force Station

Responsible for a large airbase with multiple squadrons and support units.

Group Captain
Officer

Commands a Wing, or a medium station

Senior staff role overseeing multiple squadrons or a station's operations.

Wing Commander
Officer

Commands a Squadron

Leads flight operations and personnel of an entire squadron.

Squadron Leader
Officer

Flight Commander

Leads a flight within a squadron; combines flying duties with staff responsibilities.

Flight Lieutenant
Officer

Section or flight-level responsibilities

An experienced pilot or ground-duty officer handling specific operational tasks.

Flying Officer
Officer

First operational posting after training

Entry rank for newly commissioned officers, flying or ground duty branch.

Master Warrant Officer
Airman

Senior-most non-commissioned rank, equivalent to Subedar Major

Principal advisor to the station/unit commander on matters concerning airmen.

Warrant Officer
Airman

Senior technical or administrative rank

Heads a technical section, supervising junior non-commissioned ranks.

Junior Warrant Officer
Airman

Supervisory technical duties

Assists the Warrant Officer in managing a technical section's day-to-day work.

Sergeant
Airman

Section-level supervisory rank

Leads a small team of airmen in technical or administrative duties.

Corporal
Airman

Assists the Sergeant

Handles technical and administrative tasks within a section.

Leading Aircraftman / Aircraftman
Airman

Entry-level airman ranks

Basic ranks after recruit training; the foundation of the Air Force's technical workforce.

The Coast Guard follows a Navy-style rank structure. Central Armed Police Forces (BSF, ITBP, CRPF, CISF, SSB) follow a uniform Group A officer hierarchy, with Subordinate Officers and Other Ranks below them.

Director General
Officer

Head of the Indian Coast Guard

Equivalent to a Vice Admiral; overall command of the Coast Guard's operations and policy.

Additional Director General
Officer

Senior regional/functional command

Equivalent to a Rear Admiral; heads a Coast Guard region or key directorate.

Inspector General
Officer

Regional Commander

Equivalent to a Commodore; commands a Coast Guard region.

Deputy Inspector General
Officer

District/Zone-level command

Equivalent to a Captain; oversees a Coast Guard district or major shore unit.

Commandant
Officer

Commands a Coast Guard ship or station

Equivalent to a Commander; direct command of an offshore patrol vessel or air station.

Deputy Commandant
Officer

Second-in-command aboard ship or at a station

Equivalent to a Lieutenant Commander; departmental head on larger vessels.

Assistant Commandant
Officer

First posting after training

Equivalent to a Sub Lieutenant; entry-level officer rank in the Coast Guard.

Director General
Officer

Head of the Force

Senior-most officer; overall command and policy direction of the entire force.

Additional Director General
Officer

Senior zonal command

Assists the Director General; oversees a large operational zone.

Inspector General
Officer

Frontier/Sector Commander

Commands a frontier or sector covering several battalions.

Deputy Inspector General
Officer

Range-level command

Oversees a group of battalions within a frontier/range.

Commandant
Officer

Commands a Battalion

Direct command of a battalion, typically 800–1000 personnel; equivalent to an Army Colonel.

Assistant Commandant
Officer

First posting after training (entry via CAPF exam)

Entry-level Group A officer rank; commands a company within a battalion.

Subedar Major
Subordinate Officer

Senior-most subordinate officer in a battalion

Principal advisor to the Commandant on matters concerning the rank and file.

Inspector
Subordinate Officer

Company-level administration and field command

Equivalent to a Subedar; manages a company's training and discipline.

Sub-Inspector
Subordinate Officer

Platoon-level command

Equivalent to a Naib Subedar; leads a platoon-sized unit on ground duty.

Head Constable
Other Rank

Section Commander

Equivalent to a Havildar; leads a section of constables on duty.

Naik / Lance Naik
Other Rank

Assists the Head Constable

Junior supervisory ranks within a section, similar to the Army structure.

Constable
Other Rank

Basic operational rank

Entry-level rank after training; forms the bulk of the force's strength on border/ground duty.

BHARATDEFENCE

Military History

Wars & Operations

India's major wars, border conflicts, and named military operations from 1947 to the present — high-frequency topics for SSB Lecturette, GK papers & Group Discussions.

First Kashmir War (1947–48)
UN Ceasefire

India vs Pakistan — over the accession of Jammu & Kashmir

Began after tribal raiders backed by Pakistan entered J&K following the Maharaja's accession to India. Ended with a UN-mediated ceasefire on 1 January 1949, establishing the Ceasefire Line (later the Line of Control).

Sino-Indian War (1962)
Chinese Gains

India vs China — border dispute in Aksai Chin and the North-East

Fought across the Ladakh and NEFA (now Arunachal Pradesh) sectors over disputed Himalayan borders. China declared a unilateral ceasefire on 21 November 1962 after gaining ground in Aksai Chin; led to major reforms in India's defence preparedness.

Indo-Pak War (1965)
UN Ceasefire

India vs Pakistan — triggered by Pakistan's Operation Gibraltar

Pakistan infiltrated forces into J&K hoping to spark an uprising; large tank battles took place in the Punjab/Sialkot sector, including the Battle of Asal Uttar. Ended via the Tashkent Declaration in January 1966.

Indo-Pak War / Bangladesh Liberation War (1971)
Indian Victory

India vs Pakistan — led to the creation of Bangladesh

India intervened in support of the Bangladesh liberation movement in former East Pakistan. The Indian Navy's Operation Trident & Operation Python struck Karachi harbour, while ground forces advanced on both fronts. Ended in 13 days with the surrender of over 90,000 Pakistani troops at Dhaka — the largest military surrender since World War II.

Kargil War (1999)
Indian Victory

India vs Pakistan — high-altitude conflict in the Kargil sector, J&K

Pakistani soldiers and militants occupied vacated high-altitude posts in winter. India launched Operation Vijay (ground) and Operation Safed Sagar (air) to evict them. Indian forces recaptured all key peaks, including Tiger Hill, by July 1999.

Nathu La & Cho La Clashes (1967)
Indian Hold

India vs China — Sikkim border, post-1962 tensions

Brief but intense artillery and infantry clashes along the Sikkim border. Indian troops held their positions and inflicted significant casualties, helping restore military confidence after 1962.

Sumdorong Chu Standoff (1986–87)
De-escalated

India vs China — Arunachal Pradesh border

A tense military standoff after Chinese troops set up positions in the disputed Sumdorong Chu valley. India responded with Operation Falcon, airlifting troops to the area. Resolved through diplomatic talks without major fighting.

Siachen Conflict (1984–present)
Indian Control

India vs Pakistan — world's highest battlefield, eastern Karakoram

India launched Operation Meghdoot in April 1984 to occupy key passes ahead of Pakistan, gaining control of the Saltoro Ridge. Sporadic skirmishes followed through the 1980s–90s; India has maintained control of the glacier since.

Galwan Valley Clash (2020)
Disengagement

India vs China — Eastern Ladakh, Line of Actual Control

A violent hand-to-hand clash broke out on 15 June 2020 amid a broader standoff along the LAC, with casualties on both sides. Followed by multiple rounds of military and diplomatic talks leading to phased disengagement from friction points.

Operation Polo (1948)
Annexation

Police action to integrate the princely state of Hyderabad into India

A five-day military action in September 1948 that ended the rule of the Nizam of Hyderabad and brought the state into the Indian Union.

Operation Vijay — Goa (1961)
Annexation

Military action to end Portuguese colonial rule in Goa, Daman & Diu

A swift 36-hour operation in December 1961 by the Army, Navy & Air Force that ended over 450 years of Portuguese rule on Indian soil.

Operation Meghdoot (1984)
Indian Control

Pre-emptive occupation of the Siachen Glacier

India airlifted troops onto the Saltoro Ridge just ahead of a planned Pakistani move, securing control of the glacier that continues to this day.

Operation Blue Star (1984)
Domestic Operation

Action to remove armed militants from the Golden Temple complex, Amritsar

A controversial domestic security operation in June 1984 that resulted in significant casualties and remains a sensitive chapter in India's internal security history.

Operation Pawan (1987–90)
Withdrawal

Indian Peace Keeping Force (IPKF) deployment in Sri Lanka

Indian troops were deployed to disarm Tamil militant groups under the 1987 Indo-Sri Lanka Accord. The mission faced prolonged guerrilla resistance and was withdrawn in 1990.

Operation Cactus (1988)
Successful Intervention

Rapid intervention to foil a coup attempt in the Maldives

Indian paratroopers were airlifted overnight to Malé at the Maldivian government's request, restoring order within hours and with no Indian casualties.

Operation Parakram (2001–02)
Mobilisation, No War

Large-scale military mobilisation along the Pakistan border

Launched after the December 2001 Parliament attack, this was the largest mobilisation since 1971. Forces remained deployed for nearly a year before stand-down, without a full-scale war breaking out.

Operation Sindoor (2025)
Strikes Conducted

Precision strikes on terror infrastructure in Pakistan & PoK

Launched in the early hours of 7 May 2025 in response to the 22 April Pahalgam terror attack. Indian forces struck multiple terror-linked sites; this triggered four days of cross-border strikes, drone exchanges and shelling before a ceasefire was reached on 10 May 2025.

Operation Rakshak (1990–present)
Ongoing

Long-running counter-insurgency operation in Jammu & Kashmir

An umbrella operation by the Army and central forces to counter militancy and secure the Kashmir Valley, running continuously since the early 1990s.

Operation Black Tornado (2008)
Hostages Freed

NSG counter-terror operation during the 26/11 Mumbai attacks

National Security Guard commandos cleared the Taj Mahal Palace, Oberoi Trident and Nariman House over three days, ending the siege and freeing hostages.

Operation All Out (2017–present)
Ongoing

Intensified anti-militancy operations across the Kashmir Valley

A coordinated Army-CRPF-J&K Police drive launched in 2017 to systematically target militant networks operating in the Valley.

2016 Surgical Strikes
Strikes Conducted

Cross-LoC strikes on militant launch pads after the Uri attack

Indian Army special forces crossed the Line of Control on the night of 28–29 September 2016, targeting launch pads used for infiltration, in response to the attack on an Army base in Uri.

Balakot Air Strike (2019)
Strikes Conducted

Air strike on a terror training camp in Pakistan after the Pulwama attack

IAF Mirage 2000 jets struck an alleged Jaish-e-Mohammed training facility near Balakot on 26 February 2019, days after the Pulwama bombing that killed 40 CRPF personnel. A subsequent aerial engagement led to the brief capture and return of an IAF pilot.

UN Peacekeeping Missions
Ongoing Commitment

Indian Armed Forces contribution to United Nations peacekeeping

India is among the largest historical contributors of troops to UN peacekeeping missions worldwide, with deployments across Africa, the Middle East and elsewhere over several decades.

BHARATDEFENCE

Build the Body, Build the Officer

Physical Fitness & Training

Physical Efficiency Test (PET) standards, training schedules, and conditioning advice for NDA, CDS, AFCAT, Agniveer, and SSB-bound aspirants.

NDA / CDS (at SSB, GTO outdoor tasks & informal PET)
  • 1.6 km run — under 6 minutes 30 seconds (informal benchmark used at many Service Selection Boards).
  • Skipping — 50 skips continuously.
  • Push-ups — minimum 20.
  • Sit-ups — minimum 20.
  • Chin-ups / Pull-ups — minimum 6-8.
  • Rope climbing — 3-4 metres.
  • Shuttle run / Zig-zag balance — agility component tested informally during GTO tasks.
Agniveer (Army) — Common Entry Test (CET)-qualified candidates
  • 1.6 km run — Group I: under 5 min 30 sec; Group II: 5:31-5:45 min; Group III: 5:46-6:00 min (timing decides PET marks/group, not just pass/fail).
  • Pull-ups (Beam) — Group I: 10+; Group II: 8-9; Group III: 6-7.
  • 9 feet ditch jump — qualifying (pass/fail only).
  • Zig-zag balance — qualifying, tests balance and coordination on a balance beam.
Agniveer (Navy/Air Force) & Women candidates

Navy Agniveer: 1.6 km run in 7 minutes (men) / 8 minutes (women), plus push-ups and sit-ups within set repetitions. Air Force Agniveer (Group Y/X): 1.6 km run in 8 minutes, push-ups and sit-ups qualifying. Standards are revised periodically — always check the latest official notification before appearing.

Weeks 1-4 — Base Building

Focus on aerobic base: easy 3-4 km jogs 4x/week, bodyweight circuits (push-ups, sit-ups, squats — 3 sets each), and basic mobility/stretching. Avoid heavy sprinting early to prevent shin splints and joint strain.

Weeks 5-8 — Strength & Speed

Introduce interval training (400m repeats), progressive overload on push-ups/pull-ups/sit-ups, add rope climbing practice if access available, and begin timed 1.6 km run attempts weekly to track progress.

Weeks 9-12 — Peak & Taper

Simulate exact test conditions (timed run + push-ups + sit-ups back-to-back), focus on technique refinement for pull-ups/rope climb, and taper volume in the final week before the actual test/SSB to arrive fresh, not fatigued.

General Advice
  • Hydrate well and prioritise sleep (7-8 hrs) — recovery matters as much as training volume.
  • Eat adequate protein (lean meat, dal, eggs, paneer, milk) to support muscle recovery on a student budget.
  • Train outdoors on similar terrain/surface to the actual test ground where possible.
  • Get a basic medical check before starting intense training, especially before rope climbing or rigorous running.

Beyond fitness, medical standards eliminate a large share of otherwise-qualified candidates. Common issues to address well in advance:

  • Vision — Refractive error limits vary by branch; flying branches have the strictest standards (often no LASIK acceptance or specific waiting periods post-surgery). Check branch-specific eye standards before applying.
  • BMI / Weight — Both underweight and overweight (relative to height) can lead to temporary or permanent rejection; corrective time is sometimes given for borderline cases.
  • Dental — Missing teeth beyond a permissible number, or significant gum disease, can be disqualifying; get a dental check-up and any pending treatment done well ahead of the medical exam.
  • Tattoos — Permitted only on the inner forearm (inside of elbow to wrist) and the reverse side of the palm/back of hand, per current rules; tattoos elsewhere on the body can be a rejection ground.
  • Hearing & ENT — Deviated septum, chronic sinusitis, and hearing thresholds beyond limits are commonly flagged at medicals; minor correctable conditions can sometimes be treated before the re-medical/appeal.

Always verify against the latest official notification for your specific entry, since exact medical and fitness standards are revised from time to time by the respective Service HQs.

BHARATDEFENCE

Serving with Honour, Earning with Dignity

Pay, Allowances & Pension

A look at how officers and personnel of the Indian Armed Forces are paid, the allowances layered on top of basic pay, and the pension/post-retirement framework — useful both for career decisions and GK.

Defence pay follows the 7th Central Pay Commission (CPC) Defence Pay Matrix, distinct from the civilian matrix, with separate Pay Levels for officers, JCOs/NCOs, and Other Ranks. On top of Level-based Basic Pay, every officer/JCO/OR draws Military Service Pay (MSP) — a fixed monthly addition recognising the unique nature of military service, paid in addition to all other pay and allowances.

Approximate Starting Gross (illustrative, varies by posting/allowances)
  • Lieutenant / Flying Officer / Sub Lieutenant (newly commissioned officer) — Pay Level 10, gross emoluments typically in the range of roughly ₹75,000-₹95,000+ per month including MSP and applicable allowances, before deployment-specific add-ons.
  • JCO (e.g. Naib Subedar) — Pay Level 6, with MSP, typically in the range of roughly ₹45,000-₹60,000+ gross depending on allowances.
  • Sepoy / Other Ranks (after training) — Pay Level 3, typically in the range of roughly ₹30,000-₹40,000+ gross including MSP and allowances at entry level.
  • Agniveer — A distinct, lower starting package (~₹30,000 gross in Year 1, rising annually) under the Agnipath scheme, with 30% contributed to the Agniveer Corpus Fund (matched by the government) and paid out as Seva Nidhi on completion of the 4-year tenure.

Figures are indicative and change with Dearness Allowance (DA) revisions and posting location; always check the latest official pay circular for exact current figures.

  • Siachen Allowance — Highest field allowance, for personnel posted on the Siachen Glacier, given the extreme altitude and climate.
  • High Altitude Allowance — For postings above specified altitude bands (e.g. 9,000-15,000+ feet) short of Siachen, scaled by altitude and duration.
  • Field Area Allowance / Modified Field Area Allowance — For postings in designated field and counter-insurgency areas along the borders.
  • Counter Insurgency Operations Allowance — For personnel deployed on active counter-insurgency/anti-terror operations.
  • Flying Allowance / Submarine Allowance / Sea-going Allowance — Branch-specific risk pay for aircrew, submariners, and seagoing naval personnel respectively.
  • Para Allowance / Special Forces Allowance — For qualified paratroopers and Special Forces-badged personnel.
  • Hardship Allowance (Andaman & Nicobar / Lakshadweep) — For postings to remote island territories.
  • Transport Allowance, House Rent Allowance (HRA), Dearness Allowance (DA) — Standard allowances common across government service, applicable to defence personnel as well, layered atop MSP and Basic Pay.
One Rank One Pension (OROP)

OROP ensures that retired personnel of the same rank and same length of service receive the same pension, regardless of the year of retirement, with periodic revisions to bring older pensioners' payouts in line with current rates. It was implemented from 1 July 2014 onward following years of veteran advocacy.

Pension Eligibility

Personnel completing the prescribed minimum qualifying service (commonly cited around 15 years for Other Ranks and 20 years for officers, varying by service/branch/exit type) become eligible for a service pension; those who exit before this through Short Service Commission or early release receive a gratuity/severance package rather than a full pension.

Other Post-Retirement Benefits
  • Ex-Servicemen Contributory Health Scheme (ECHS) — Lifetime medical cover for veterans and dependents at empanelled and service hospitals/polyclinics.
  • Canteen Stores Department (CSD) facility — Continued access to subsidised CSD canteens for daily essentials.
  • Disability Pension — Additional pension component for those invalided out due to service-attributable disability, on top of regular service pension where applicable.
  • Reservation in Government Jobs — Ex-servicemen quota reservations in central/state government recruitment, plus priority access for Agniveers who complete their 4-year tenure (as separately announced by several states/CAPFs).
BHARATDEFENCE

Breaking New Ground

Women in Armed Forces

From the 1992 entry of the first women officers to the 2021 NDA verdict and Permanent Commission — how women's roles in the Indian Armed Forces have expanded, and where the current entry pathways stand.

  • 1888 — Indian Military Nursing Service (IMNS) established, the earliest formal entry of women into uniformed military service in India.
  • 1992 — Women commissioned as officers for the first time in the Indian Army (non-medical streams) and the IAF, initially via Short Service Commission (SSC) only, in select non-combat branches.
  • 1994 — Women officers inducted into the Indian Navy.
  • 2015 — Indian Air Force opens the fighter pilot stream to women, making the IAF among the first of the three services to allow women in a frontline combat flying role.
  • 2020 — Supreme Court of India directs Permanent Commission (PC) for women officers in the Army across ten streams, ending the SSC-only ceiling that had forced many capable women officers out after 14 years of service.
  • 2021 — Supreme Court permits women candidates to sit the NDA entrance exam for the first time, following a writ petition; the first batch of women cadets joined NDA Khadakwasla in 2022.
  • 2023 — First woman officer takes command of an Indian Army unit on the Line of Control; Agniveer scheme opens women's entry into Other Ranks for the first time in the Army's history (Corps of Military Police).
Officer Entries
  • NDA — Open to women since the 2022 intake (Army, Navy, Air Force wings, subject to branch-specific medical/role restrictions).
  • CDS — Women can apply via IMA, OTA (SSC Non-Tech), AFA, and INA entries, depending on the specific notification for that CDS cycle.
  • AFCAT — Open to women for Flying, Technical, and Ground Duty branches of the IAF, including the fighter stream.
  • JAG / NCC Special Entry / Technical & Non-Tech SSC entries — Open across Army, Navy and Air Force for women graduates/postgraduates in eligible disciplines.
  • Permanent Commission (PC) — Available in the Army across streams such as Army Air Defence, Signals, Engineers, Army Aviation, EME, AOC, Intelligence Corps, and the Judge Advocate General's branch, among others, following the 2020 Supreme Court direction.
Other Ranks / Agniveer

Women are currently inducted as Agniveers in the Corps of Military Police, the first Other Ranks-level combat-support entry opened to women in the Indian Army.

Restrictions That Still Apply

Direct combat infantry/armoured roles for women remain under policy review across services; submarine postings for women in the Navy and certain front-line ground-combat appointments are not yet open, though the trend over the past decade has consistently been toward expanding access.

  • Punita Arora — First woman Lieutenant General-equivalent rank holder, having served in both the Army and Navy medical services.
  • Padmavathy Bandopadhyay — First woman Air Marshal in the IAF.
  • Avani Chaturvedi, Bhawana Kanth, Mohana Singh — Among the IAF's first women fighter pilots, commissioned in 2016.
  • Shivangi Singh — First woman Rafale fighter pilot in the IAF.
  • Gunjan Saxena, Srividya Rajan — Among the earliest women pilots to fly in an actual combat zone (Kargil War, 1999), in the Air Force's helicopter/transport stream.
  • Priya Jhingan — India's first woman officer commissioned into the Indian Army (1993 batch).
BHARATDEFENCE

Pomp, Protocol & Remembrance

Parades & Ceremonies

The major ceremonial events of India's military calendar — useful GK for Lecturette, Group Discussion, and current affairs rounds at SSB.

Held on 26 January on Kartavya Path, New Delhi, marking the date the Constitution of India came into force in 1950. The parade begins with the Prime Minister laying a wreath at the National War Memorial, followed by the President's arrival and the National Anthem, a 21-gun salute, and the hoisting of the National Flag.

Sequence
  • Tri-Services Marching Contingents — Army, Navy, and Air Force contingents march in order of seniority, followed by NCC, Police, and paramilitary contingents.
  • Mechanised Columns — Tanks, missile systems, and military hardware showcase India's indigenous and acquired defence capability.
  • Flypast — IAF aircraft formations conclude the military segment with a flypast over Kartavya Path.
  • State Tableaux — Floats from states/UTs and central ministries showcase cultural and developmental themes.
  • Gallantry Award Presentation — The President honours recipients of select gallantry awards, and children awarded the National Bravery Award also take part in the parade.

Held on 29 January at Vijay Chowk, New Delhi, formally concluding the Republic Day celebrations. Bands of the Army, Navy, Air Force, and Delhi Police perform martial and patriotic tunes, with the ceremony traditionally ending as the sun sets and the National Flag is lowered for the day — symbolising troops "retreating" from the battlefield at dusk in old military tradition. Recent editions have included Indian classical instrument-based compositions alongside Western military band traditions, and a laser-light/drone show has been added in some years.

Independence Day (15 August) commemorates India's freedom from British rule in 1947. Unlike Republic Day's full tri-services marching parade, the main national ceremony is centred on the Prime Minister's flag hoisting and address, with a distinct flag protocol and a separate, smaller-scale ceremonial structure.

Key Protocol Difference from Republic Day
  • Who hoists the flag — On Independence Day, the Prime Minister hoists the National Flag at the Red Fort, Delhi (since the flag is "unfurled" — already at the top of the pole, signifying a free nation that no longer needs to raise its flag from the ground). On Republic Day, the President "hoists" the flag (raises it from the bottom of the pole) at Kartavya Path, since the President is the ceremonial Head of State and Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces.
  • Venue — Red Fort (Independence Day) vs Kartavya Path (Republic Day).
  • Address — The Prime Minister delivers the nation-facing address on Independence Day from the Red Fort ramparts; on Republic Day, the President's address to the nation is broadcast separately (on the eve, 25 January).
Sequence of Events
  • Prime Minister arrives at Red Fort and inspects the Guard of Honour, comprising a composite tri-services contingent and the Delhi Police.
  • Flag unfurling, National Anthem, and a 21-gun salute fired by Army gunners.
  • Prime Minister's address to the nation from the Red Fort ramparts, reviewing the year's achievements and announcing key initiatives.
  • School children's cultural programmes and patriotic song performances follow in many editions.
  • Tri-colour balloons/kites are released in some years; coordinated security cover is provided by Delhi Police, NSG, and the Special Protection Group given the high-profile gathering.
Nationwide Observance

Flag hoisting ceremonies are held at state capitals (by Chief Ministers/Governors), government offices, schools, and institutions across the country, alongside the "Har Ghar Tiranga" movement encouraging citizens to hoist the flag at homes. The Independence Day flag protocol — including the right to fly the flag at homes and offices — is governed by the Flag Code of India, 2002, as covered in the Flags & Heraldry section.

National War Memorial (NWM)

Inaugurated in 2019 near India Gate, the National War Memorial honours over 25,000 Indian soldiers who died in conflicts since Independence (1947 war, 1961 Goa, 1962, 1965, 1971, 1999 Kargil, and UN peacekeeping operations), with their names inscribed on granite tablets. Its design includes four concentric circles — Amar Chakra, Veerta Chakra, Tyag Chakra, and Rakshak Chakra.

Amar Jawan Jyoti

Originally lit beneath India Gate in 1972 after the 1971 war as an eternal flame honouring the unknown soldier, the flame was merged into the National War Memorial in 2022, consolidating the symbolic eternal flame at the new memorial site.

Other Major Memorials
  • Kargil War Memorial (Dras) — Honours soldiers of the 1999 Kargil War, inscribed with the motto "Yeh Dil Maange More."
  • INS Vikrant Memorial / Naval War Memorials — Commemorate the Navy's role across conflicts, notably the 1971 war's Operation Trident.
BHARATDEFENCE

One Day, One Force, One Story

Forces Days & Raising Days

Every branch of India's defence and security apparatus marks its own day — commemorating a founding date or a historic act of valour. High-frequency GK for exams and SSB Lecturette.

Why 15 January

Army Day commemorates 15 January 1949, the day Field Marshal K. M. Cariappa took over as the first Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army from the last British Commander-in-Chief, General Sir Francis Robert Roy Bucher — marking the full Indianisation of the Army's top command, just over a year after Independence.

How It's Celebrated
  • A ceremonial parade is held at the Army Parade Ground, Delhi Cantonment (the venue rotated to different Command Headquarters cities starting 2023, e.g. Bengaluru, Lucknow, to take the event beyond Delhi).
  • The Chief of Army Staff takes the salute, reviews marching contingents, and presents gallantry/distinguished service awards.
  • Equipment displays, military band performances, and tableaux showcasing the Army's history and modernisation are featured.
  • Wreaths are laid at the National War Memorial in honour of fallen soldiers.
Why 4 December

Navy Day marks the anniversary of Operation Trident (4 December 1971), the daring missile-boat strike by the Indian Navy on Karachi harbour during the 1971 Indo-Pak War. Soviet-supplied Osa-class missile boats — INS Nipat, INS Nirghat, and INS Veer — used Soviet P-15 Termit (Styx) missiles to devastating effect, sinking/damaging Pakistani warships and fuel depots, in what remains one of the most successful surface-action operations in the Navy's history.

How It's Celebrated
  • Operational demonstrations ("Operational Demonstration" / President's Fleet Review in select years) showcase live firing, fly-bys, and amphibious assault drills, usually at a coastal city (Visakhapatnam, Mumbai, Goa, Puri have hosted in different years).
  • Wreath-laying ceremonies honour personnel lost during Operation Trident and Operation Python (the follow-up Karachi strike, 8-9 December 1971).
  • The Chief of Naval Staff addresses the press on the Navy's modernisation, indigenous shipbuilding progress, and operational readiness.
Why 8 October

The Indian Air Force was officially established on 8 October 1932 as an auxiliary air force of the British Empire, with its first flight (No. 1 Squadron) becoming operational in April 1933. The IAF dropped "Royal" from its title in 1950 after India became a republic.

How It's Celebrated
  • A grand flypast and aerobatic display is held annually, typically at Hindon Air Force Station (Ghaziabad/NCR) or other Air Force Stations on a rotational basis, featuring fighter jets (Tejas, Su-30MKI, Rafale), transport aircraft, and helicopters.
  • The Surya Kiran aerobatic display team performs formation flying as a highlight.
  • The Chief of the Air Staff takes the salute and presents awards for distinguished service.
  • A "Tribute Formation" flypast often honours fallen IAF personnel.
Why 1 February

The Indian Coast Guard was established on 1 February 1977 under the Coast Guard Act, 1978 (the force was raised in 1977, with the Act formalising it the following year), as a distinct armed force responsible for maritime law enforcement, search and rescue, and protection of India's coastline and Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ), operating under the Ministry of Defence.

How It's Celebrated
  • Raising Day parades are held at Coast Guard Regional Headquarters (Mumbai, Chennai, Visakhapatnam, Port Blair) with ship/aircraft displays.
  • Search-and-rescue and anti-pollution demonstrations showcase operational readiness.
  • Awards for gallantry and meritorious service (Tatrakshak Medals) are presented by the Director General, Indian Coast Guard.

The Border Security Force was raised on 1 December 1965, in the aftermath of the 1965 Indo-Pak War, which exposed the need for a dedicated, centrally-controlled border-guarding force distinct from state police. BSF today guards the India-Pakistan and India-Bangladesh borders. The day is marked with parades at the BSF Academy (Tekanpur, Madhya Pradesh) and force headquarters, along with award presentations and martyrs' tribute ceremonies.

The Central Reserve Police Force traces its lineage to 27 July 1825, when it was raised as the Crown Representative's Police; it was reorganised and renamed the Central Reserve Police Force after Independence under the CRPF Act, 1949. India's largest CAPF, it specialises in internal security, counter-insurgency (notably anti-Naxal operations via its CoBRA units), and election/VIP security duties. 21 October is separately observed as Police Commemoration Day nationally, honouring the 10 CRPF personnel killed in the 1959 Hot Springs (Ladakh) clash with Chinese troops — distinct from the force's own Raising Day.

The Indo-Tibetan Border Police was raised on 24 October 1962, in the immediate aftermath of the 1962 Sino-Indian War, to guard the high-altitude Himalayan border with Tibet/China and conduct mountain rescue operations. ITBP personnel are specially trained for extreme high-altitude warfare and have been central to mountaineering expeditions and disaster-relief operations in the Himalayan region.

The Sashastra Seema Bal was raised on 20 December 1963 (originally as the Special Service Bureau, in the aftermath of the 1962 war, for border-area civic and intelligence work) and was reorganised in 2001 as an armed border-guarding force for the India-Nepal and India-Bhutan frontiers, becoming a full CAPF in 2003.

The Central Industrial Security Force was raised on 10 March 1969 to provide integrated security to key public sector industrial undertakings; its mandate has since expanded to cover airports, metro systems, nuclear and space installations, heritage monuments, and private-sector critical infrastructure under the CISF (Amendment) Act, 2009.

India's oldest paramilitary force, the Assam Rifles traces its origin to 24 March 1835, when it was raised as the "Cachar Levy" to protect British tea estates and frontier settlements in the Northeast. It has been administratively under the Ministry of Home Affairs and operationally under the Army's command since Independence, earning the title "Sentinel of the North East" and "Friends of the Hill People" for its century-and-a-half of frontier service.

The National Security Guard was raised on 16 October 1984, in the wake of Operation Blue Star and the assassination of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi, as India's premier federal counter-terrorism force, modelled partly on Germany's GSG 9. It is famously associated with Operation Black Tornado, the 2008 Mumbai (26/11) terror attack response.

The Railway Protection Force, responsible for the security of railway property, passenger areas, and passengers, traces its statutory origin to the Railway Protection Force Act, 1957, with 20 September observed as Raising Day; it was granted full armed-force status (powers of arrest/investigation) under the RPF (Amendment) Act, 1985 — though it is sometimes grouped separately from the core CAPFs since it falls under the Ministry of Railways rather than the Ministry of Home Affairs.

The Territorial Army was raised on 9 October 1949 under the Territorial Army Act, 1948, as a volunteer reserve force allowing civilians to serve part-time, freeing the Regular Army for frontline duties during emergencies and providing trained manpower for static and logistic roles.

BHARATDEFENCE

Atmanirbhar Bharat in Defence

Defence Industry & DRDO

India's push toward indigenous defence manufacturing — the key organisations, flagship platforms, and the policy push behind "Make in India" for defence. High-yield current affairs and Lecturette material.

DRDO was formed in 1958 by merging the Technical Development Establishment and the Directorate of Technical Development & Production with the Defence Science Organisation, and today operates over 50 laboratories under the Ministry of Defence, working on missiles, aeronautics, armaments, electronics, and materials science.

Flagship DRDO Programmes
  • Agni series — Intermediate to intercontinental-range ballistic missiles (Agni-I to Agni-V/Agni-Prime), forming the backbone of India's land-based strategic deterrent.
  • Prithvi & BrahMos — Prithvi is a short-range tactical ballistic missile; BrahMos (a DRDO-NPO Mashinostroyeniya joint venture with Russia) is a supersonic cruise missile fielded by the Army, Navy and Air Force.
  • Akash & QRSAM — Indigenous surface-to-air missile systems for area and quick-reaction air defence.
  • Tejas (LCA) — Light Combat Aircraft developed with DRDO/ADA and manufactured by HAL; inducted into the IAF and exported interest from several countries.
  • Arjun MBT — Indigenous Main Battle Tank programme, developed over multiple variants (Mk1, Mk1A).
  • INS Arihant-class — Indigenous nuclear-powered ballistic missile submarine programme, developed with DRDO support, completing India's nuclear triad.
  • Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) — Bengaluru; manufactures Tejas, Su-30MKI (under licence), HAL Dhruv/Rudra helicopters, and the Light Combat Helicopter (Prachand).
  • Bharat Electronics Limited (BEL) — Develops radars, electronic warfare systems, communication equipment, and sonar systems for all three services.
  • Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited (MDL) — Mumbai; builds destroyers, frigates, and Scorpène-class submarines (Project-75) for the Navy.
  • Garden Reach Shipbuilders & Engineers (GRSE) — Kolkata; builds patrol vessels, corvettes, and survey ships.
  • Bharat Dynamics Limited (BDL) — Hyderabad; manufactures guided missiles including Akash and Konkurs-M anti-tank missiles.
  • Munitions India Limited & other Ordnance Factory Board successor companies — Manufacture ammunition, small arms, and military vehicles, restructured from the erstwhile Ordnance Factory Board in 2021.
Positive Indigenisation Lists

The Ministry of Defence has progressively notified multiple "Positive Indigenisation Lists" — categories of weapons/platforms (initially several hundred items, expanded across successive lists) that can only be procured from domestic sources after a specified timeline, pushing the Armed Forces and industry toward indigenous sourcing.

iDEX & Innovation

Innovations for Defence Excellence (iDEX) is a Ministry of Defence initiative to fund and mentor start-ups and MSMEs working on defence and dual-use technologies, run through the Defence Innovation Organisation.

Defence Exports

India's annual defence exports have grown substantially over the past decade, driven by platforms such as BrahMos (exported to the Philippines), Akash systems, artillery guns, and patrol vessels, as part of the broader push to position India as a credible defence exporter rather than a predominantly import-dependent market.

Why This Matters for SSB/GK

Defence indigenisation is a recurring Lecturette and GD topic — be ready to discuss the rationale (strategic autonomy, forex savings, employment generation), the challenges (technology gaps, R&D timelines, private sector participation), and examples of recent indigenous platforms inducted into service.

BHARATDEFENCE

Youth in Uniform

NCC & Scouts / Guides

Two of India's largest uniformed youth movements — feeding leadership, discipline, and a service mindset into both civilian life and the armed forces. For defence aspirants, NCC offers concrete entry advantages, while Bharat Scouts & Guides builds the same foundational character.

Establishment

The National Cadet Corps (NCC) was formally raised on 16 April 1948 under the National Cadet Corps Act, 1948, on the recommendation of the H. N. Kunzru Committee (1946). It traces its institutional roots to the University Officers Training Corps (UOTC) set up in 1942 during the Second World War. Headquartered in New Delhi under the Ministry of Defence, NCC is today the world's largest uniformed youth organisation, with cadets across schools and colleges.

Motto & Aim

NCC's motto is "Ekta aur Anushasan" (Unity and Discipline). Its three declared aims are: (1) to develop character, comradeship, discipline, secular outlook, spirit of adventure, and ideals of selfless service among the youth; (2) to create a pool of organised, trained, and motivated youth with leadership qualities; and (3) to provide a suitable environment to motivate young Indians to take up a career in the armed forces.

Three Wings
  • Army Wing — infantry, armoured, artillery, signals, engineers, EME, AMC, and other allied subjects.
  • Naval Wing — seamanship, navigation, communication, ship modelling, sailing.
  • Air Wing — aeromodelling, gliding, micro-light flying, principles of flight.
Organisation

NCC is administered through 17 State Directorates, with Group Headquarters at city level and Battalions/Units attached to schools and colleges. The Director General NCC (DGNCC), a serving Lieutenant General, heads the organisation at the national level.

Training Activities

Cadet training is structured across institutional training (drill, weapon training, map reading, field craft) and adventure activities (trekking, mountaineering, sailing, gliding, parasailing). Cadets also receive instruction on the Constitution, national integration, social service, and disaster management.

Camps
  • Annual Training Camps (ATC) — basic 10-day camps held at directorate level.
  • Combined Annual Training Camps (CATC) — joint Army/Navy/Air wing training.
  • Thal Sainik Camp, Nau Sainik Camp, Vayu Sainik Camp — service-specific competitive camps.
  • Republic Day Camp (RDC), New Delhi — the pinnacle camp; selected cadets march in the Republic Day Parade and are reviewed by the Prime Minister.
  • Ek Bharat Shreshtha Bharat (EBSB) Camps & Youth Exchange Programmes (YEP) with foreign countries.
Social & National Service

NCC cadets contribute to blood donation drives, tree plantation, Swachh Bharat campaigns, anti-drug rallies, disaster relief (floods, earthquakes), traffic management, and assistance to civil authorities during major events.

Divisions of NCC
  • Junior Division / Junior Wing (JD/JW) — schools, classes 8 to 12 (boys and girls).
  • Senior Division / Senior Wing (SD/SW) — colleges and universities (boys and girls).
Eligibility

Open to Indian citizens (and subjects of Nepal/Bhutan in certain cases) studying in a school or college that has an NCC unit. Age: 12 to 18.5 years for JD/JW; 18 to 26 years for SD/SW. Candidates must be medically fit.

Selection Process
  1. Apply through the institution: NCC is offered through schools/colleges affiliated to an NCC unit. Cadets enrol via the school NCC officer (ANO — Associate NCC Officer).
  2. Physical Fitness Test: push-ups, sit-ups, running, basic flexibility — standards differ for boys/girls and division.
  3. Medical Examination: general fitness check; no major disability, vision within prescribed limits.
  4. Interview / Aptitude: short personal interaction by the ANO and NCC officer; assesses motivation and attitude.
  5. Enrolment & Uniform: selected cadets are enrolled, issued uniform, and start parade training.
Certificates
  • Certificate "A" — Junior Division cadets after 2 years and one ATC.
  • Certificate "B" — Senior Division cadets after 2nd year, one ATC.
  • Certificate "C" — final-year SD/SW cadets after RDC/CATC or equivalent. Highest NCC certificate.
NCC Special Entry Scheme (Indian Army)

NCC "C" certificate holders with minimum 50% marks in graduation are eligible to apply for the NCC Special Entry Scheme directly — they are exempted from the written CDS examination and called directly for the SSB interview. Around 50 vacancies (men + women) are reserved each year for OTA Chennai.

Air Force & Navy

NCC "C" certificate (Air/Naval Wing) holders receive bonus marks/priority for AFCAT and Indian Navy entries. The IAF also reserves dedicated vacancies for NCC Air Wing "C" certificate holders in the flying branch.

Other Advantages
  • Bonus marks in police, paramilitary, and many state government recruitments.
  • Reservation/preference in some PSU and government job interviews.
  • Exemption from certain physical tests in central armed police forces.
  • Strong head-start in the SSB Interview — drill, OLQs, group tasks, lecturette all feel familiar.
  • Adventure exposure (mountaineering, gliding) that strengthens SSB GTO and Personal Interview.
Establishment

The Bharat Scouts & Guides (BS&G) was formed on 7 November 1950 as the unified national scouting organisation of independent India, by merging the Boy Scouts Association in India, the Hindustan Scouts Association, the Girl Guides, and other regional bodies. India had been part of the world scouting movement since 1909, when scouting was introduced by Lord Baden-Powell's followers.

Motto & Promise

Motto: "Be Prepared" (Taiyar). The Scout/Guide Promise commits the member to do their duty to God and country, to help other people, and to obey the Scout/Guide Law (10 points covering trustworthiness, loyalty, helpfulness, courtesy, courage, discipline, thrift, and respect for nature).

Sections by Age
  • Cubs / Bulbuls — 5 to 10 years
  • Scouts / Guides — 10 to 17 years
  • Rovers / Rangers — 17 to 25 years
Recognition

BS&G is the official Indian member of the World Organization of the Scout Movement (WOSM) and the World Association of Girl Guides and Girl Scouts (WAGGGS).

Activities & Work

Scouts and Guides train in camping, pioneering, first aid, map & compass, hiking, signalling, community service, environment conservation, and disaster preparedness. They participate in jamborees (state, national, and world-level), rallies, and service projects (cleanliness drives, tree plantation, awareness campaigns, helping at religious gatherings such as Kumbh Mela).

Joining Process
  1. Through school/institution: most government and many private schools have a registered Scouts/Guides unit. Students enrol with the Scout Master / Guide Captain.
  2. Open Troops: for children/youth whose school does not have a unit; one can join district-level open troops through the State BS&G office.
  3. Registration & Investiture: after probation and learning basic Promise/Law, the candidate is formally invested as a Scout/Guide.
  4. Progressive Badges: Pravesh → Pratham Sopan → Dwitiya Sopan → Tritiya Sopan → Rajya Puraskar (Governor's Award) → Rashtrapati Puraskar (President's Award — highest).
Why It Helps Defence Aspirants

Scouting builds the exact qualities tested at the SSB — initiative, planning, teamwork, outdoor stamina, and a service mindset. Rashtrapati Puraskar holders also enjoy preferential weightage in many state recruitments. Combined with NCC, scouting gives a strong foundation in leadership, discipline, and adventure long before formal defence training begins.

BHARATDEFENCE

From School to Service

Sainik Schools, Military Schools & Training Academies

A complete picture of India's feeder schools and premier officer-training academies — when they were established, what they actually do, and exactly how a candidate gets in.

Establishment

Sainik Schools were founded in 1961 on the initiative of V. K. Krishna Menon, then Defence Minister, to democratise officer entry into the armed forces and broaden the regional and social base of the officer cadre. The schools are run by the autonomous Sainik Schools Society under the Ministry of Defence, with each school functioning under a serving Principal (usually a retired armed forces officer).

Network

From the original cluster of five in 1961, the system has grown to 33+ Sainik Schools across nearly every State and UT. Since 2021, the government has also approved 100 "New Pattern" Sainik Schools in affiliation with the Sainik Schools Society, covering classes 6 to 12 with both government and private partner schools.

What They Do (Aim & Work)
  • Prepare boys (and now girls) academically, mentally, and physically for entry into the National Defence Academy (NDA) and the Indian Naval Academy (INA).
  • CBSE-affiliated, fully residential schools with structured academics from classes 6 to 12.
  • Daily PT, drill, games, NCC, adventure activities, and character training alongside regular studies.
  • Strong focus on values: integrity, discipline, leadership, and patriotism.
Selection Process
  1. AISSEE (All India Sainik Schools Entrance Examination) — conducted by the National Testing Agency (NTA), usually in January, for admission to Class 6 and Class 9.
  2. Eligibility: Class 6 — 10 to 12 years; Class 9 — 13 to 15 years (as on 31 March of admission year). Both boys and girls are eligible (girls' admission progressively expanded from 2021).
  3. Written Exam: OMR-based — Mathematics, GK, Language, and Intelligence (Class 6); Maths, Science, English, Social Studies, GK (Class 9).
  4. Medical Examination: conducted at a military hospital — vision, hearing, height/weight, general fitness.
  5. Final Merit: based on written marks; admission confirmed only after medical fitness and document verification.
Path After School

Sainik School cadets sit for the UPSC NDA examination after class 12. Historically, a very high share of NDA cadets come from Sainik Schools and RIMC/RMS.

Establishment

The Rashtriya Military Schools (RMS), formerly King George's Royal Indian Military Colleges (later Military Schools), were originally set up by the British in 1925 — the first at Chail (Himachal Pradesh, 1922), followed by Jhelum, Jalandhar, Ajmer, Belgaum, Bangalore, and Dholpur. After independence they were renamed Military Schools (1952), and in 2007 redesignated as Rashtriya Military Schools. They are administered directly by the Army Welfare Education Society under the Ministry of Defence.

Active Schools

There are currently five RMS: Chail, Ajmer, Belgaum, Bangalore, and Dholpur. All are CBSE-affiliated, fully residential, and admit students from Class 6 (and limited Class 9 vacancies).

What They Do (Aim & Work)
  • Prepare cadets for the NDA and other defence services entries.
  • Provide quality public-school education with strong emphasis on character, discipline, sports, and leadership.
  • Focus on all-round development: academics, PT, NCC, horse-riding (where applicable), adventure camps, and house-system character training.
Selection Process
  1. Common Entrance Test (CET) — conducted nationally for admission to Class 6 and Class 9.
  2. Eligibility: Class 6 — 10 to 12 years; Class 9 — 13 to 15 years (as on 31 March of admission year). Both boys and girls eligible (girls' intake progressively introduced).
  3. Written Exam Subjects: English, Mathematics, Intelligence, and General Knowledge (Class 6); English, Maths, Science, Social Studies, Hindi (Class 9).
  4. Viva Voce / Interview: shortlisted candidates undergo an oral interaction.
  5. Medical Examination: at a military hospital, similar standards to Sainik Schools.
  6. Final Merit: combined score (written + viva) plus medical fitness.
Establishment

The Rashtriya Indian Military College (RIMC) was founded on 13 March 1922 at Dehradun as the "Prince of Wales Royal Indian Military College", specifically to Indianise the officer corps of the British Indian Army. It is the oldest feeder institution to the NDA and remains a Category "A" establishment of the Indian Army.

What It Does
  • Provides residential public-school education (CBSE) from Class 8 to Class 12 to a small, highly selected batch of cadets.
  • Prepares cadets exclusively for the NDA and INA.
  • Strong emphasis on horsemanship, drill, adventure, and intellectual rigour; cadets follow an army-style routine.
Selection Process
  1. Entrance Exam conducted twice a year (June and December) for admission in January and July sessions to Class 8 only.
  2. Eligibility: 11.5 to 13 years (as on 1 January / 1 July of admission). Boys and girls (girls admitted since 2022).
  3. Written Exam: English, Mathematics, and General Knowledge.
  4. Viva Voce: in-depth interview to assess personality, awareness, and motivation.
  5. Medical Examination: stringent service-standard medical at a military hospital.

RIMC has one of the highest NDA conversion rates of any school in India and is considered among the most prestigious feeders to the Indian armed forces.

Establishment

The National Defence Academy (NDA) was formally established on 7 December 1954 at Khadakwasla, near Pune, Maharashtra. It is the world's first tri-service academy, where cadets of the Army, Navy, and Air Force train together for the first three years of their officer career.

What It Does
  • 3-year residential training programme covering academics (B.A./B.Sc./B.Sc. Computer Science / B.Tech for IAF flying cadets — affiliated to Jawaharlal Nehru University) and rigorous military training.
  • After NDA, cadets proceed to their service-specific academy: IMA (Army), INA (Navy), or AFA (Air Force) for the final year of pre-commission training.
  • Focus on physical fitness, drill, weapon training, fieldcraft, watermanship, gliding, and leadership.
Selection Process
  1. UPSC NDA & NA Examination — held twice a year (NDA I & NDA II).
  2. Eligibility: 16.5 to 19.5 years (as on the date specified in the notification); unmarried; 10+2 with Physics, Chemistry & Mathematics (for Navy/Air Force/Army-Tech) or 10+2 in any stream (Army). Both boys and girls eligible (girls inducted from NDA-148 course, 2022).
  3. Written Exam: Paper I — Mathematics (300 marks); Paper II — General Ability Test (English + GK, 600 marks).
  4. SSB Interview: 5-day Services Selection Board procedure — Screening (OIR + PPDT), Psychology, GTO tasks, Personal Interview, Conference (900 marks).
  5. Medical Examination: by a Special Medical Board.
  6. Final Merit: Written + SSB; then medical fitness and merit-based service allotment.
Establishment

The Indian Military Academy (IMA) was established on 1 October 1932 at Dehradun, following the recommendations of the Indian Sandhurst Committee (Skeen Committee, 1925), to train Indians for King's Commission in the army — replacing reliance on Sandhurst, UK. The first batch of 40 Gentlemen Cadets ("The Pioneers") passed out in 1934, including officers who later became Field Marshals and General Officers.

What It Does
  • Final pre-commission training academy for the Indian Army officer cadre.
  • Duration: ~1 year for cadets from NDA, ~1.5 years for direct entries (CDSE, TGC, etc.).
  • Cadets are designated Gentlemen Cadets (GCs) and live by the IMA Credo (Field Marshal Chetwode Hall): "The safety, honour and welfare of your country come first, always and every time…"
  • On successful completion, GCs are commissioned as Lieutenants in the Indian Army at the historic Passing Out Parade.
Entry Routes

NDA → IMA; CDSE (UPSC) → IMA; TGC (Technical Graduate Course) → IMA; UES (University Entry Scheme) → IMA; NCC Special Entry → OTA Chennai (sister academy).

Establishment

The Officers Training Academy (OTA), Chennai was established on 15 January 1963 to train officers for Short Service Commission (SSC) in the Indian Army. A second academy, OTA Gaya (Bihar), was raised in 2011 to expand officer-training capacity.

What It Does
  • Trains both men and women as Short Service Commissioned officers (49-week course).
  • Curriculum mirrors IMA — drill, weapons, tactics, leadership, military history, physical training, fieldcraft, plus Service Subjects.
  • Successful cadets are commissioned as Lieutenants, initially on Short Service terms (extendable / convertible to Permanent Commission as per policy).
Entry Routes
  • CDSE (UPSC) → OTA — Short Service Commission (Men & Women).
  • NCC Special Entry → OTA — for NCC "C" certificate holders.
  • SSC Tech (Men & Women) — for engineering graduates.
  • JAG (Judge Advocate General) Entry — for law graduates.
Establishment

The present Indian Naval Academy (INA) at Ezhimala, Kerala was commissioned on 8 January 2009 by the then Prime Minister, replacing the older naval academy at Goa. It is the largest naval academy in Asia and trains officer cadets for the Indian Navy and Indian Coast Guard.

What It Does
  • Conducts the 4-year B.Tech Naval Orientation Course (NOC) for 10+2 (B.Tech) Cadet Entry — affiliated to Jawaharlal Nehru University.
  • Conducts the Naval Orientation Course (Extended/Regular) for graduates entering through CDSE and other entries.
  • Imparts seamanship, navigation, marine engineering, electrical engineering, weapons, and naval traditions, along with rigorous physical and watermanship training.
Entry Routes

NDA → INA; 10+2 (B.Tech) Cadet Entry → INA; CDSE (UPSC) → INA; UES, SSC (General Service/Tech/Pilot/Observer/Logistics/Education/Law) → INA; NCC Special Entry (Navy).

Establishment

The Air Force Academy (AFA) at Dundigal, Hyderabad (Telangana) was established on 11 October 1969 as the premier training institution of the Indian Air Force. It consolidated earlier IAF training establishments (notably at Begumpet and Jodhpur) into a single integrated academy.

What It Does
  • Final pre-commission training academy for all IAF officer branches: Flying, Technical (Aeronautical Engineering — Mechanical & Electronics), and Ground Duty (Administration, Logistics, Accounts, Education, Meteorology).
  • Duration: 74 weeks for most entries; Flying Branch cadets continue stage-wise flying training on Pilatus PC-7 Mk II (basic), Kiran (intermediate), and Hawk (advanced) trainers.
  • Cadets are commissioned as Flying Officers at the impressive AFA Combined Graduation Parade.
Entry Routes

NDA → AFA (Flying); CDSE (UPSC) → AFA (Flying / Ground Duty); AFCAT → AFA (Flying / Tech / Ground Duty); NCC Special Entry (Air Wing) → AFA (Flying); NDA / 10+2 TES (Technical) for technical streams.

Establishment & Role

The Indian Coast Guard Academy (ICGA) is being developed at Mangaluru, Karnataka; until full operationalisation, Coast Guard officer training is carried out at the Indian Naval Academy, Ezhimala alongside Navy cadets. The ICG itself was raised on 1 February 1977 and formally constituted under the Coast Guard Act, 1978.

Work
  • Maritime law enforcement, search and rescue, anti-smuggling, anti-piracy, marine environment protection, and coastal security.
  • Officer cadets share early training with the Navy and then specialise in Coast Guard roles.
Selection

Entries include Assistant Commandant (General Duty, GD Pilot, Tech, Law, Commercial Pilot License Entry). Candidates apply through the Indian Coast Guard's central recruitment portal, undergo a written exam (Preliminary Selection), Coast Guard Preliminary & Final Selection Boards (Mental Ability & Physical Tests, Psychological Tests, Group Tasks, Interview), followed by medical examination — broadly similar to SSB but conducted by the ICG.

BHARATDEFENCE

Speak the Language

Glossary of Defence Acronyms

Quick-reference decoding of the abbreviations used throughout exams, SSB documentation, and this site — grouped by theme for fast lookup.

  • PVC — Param Vir Chakra (highest wartime gallantry award)
  • MVC — Maha Vir Chakra
  • VrC — Vir Chakra
  • AC — Ashok Chakra (highest peacetime gallantry award)
  • KC — Kirti Chakra
  • SC — Shaurya Chakra
  • SM — Sena Medal
  • VM — Vishisht Seva Medal
  • PVSM / AVSM — Param Vishisht Seva Medal / Ati Vishisht Seva Medal (distinguished service awards)
  • SSB — Services Selection Board
  • OLQ — Officer Like Qualities
  • PIQ — Personal Information Questionnaire
  • OIR — Officer Intelligence Rating (Stage I screening test)
  • PPDT — Picture Perception & Discussion Test
  • TAT — Thematic Apperception Test
  • WAT — Word Association Test
  • SRT — Situation Reaction Test
  • SDT — Self Description Test
  • GTO — Group Testing Officer (conducts outdoor tasks)
  • PGT / GGT / HGT — Progressive Group Task / Group Obstacle Task / Half Group Task
  • GD — Group Discussion
  • GPE — Group Planning Exercise
  • CO — Command Task
  • FGT — Final Group Task
  • NDA — National Defence Academy
  • CDS — Combined Defence Services
  • AFCAT — Air Force Common Admission Test
  • TES — Technical Entry Scheme
  • TGC — Technical Graduate Course
  • UES — University Entry Scheme
  • SSC / PC — Short Service Commission / Permanent Commission
  • IMA / OTA / AFA / INA — Indian Military Academy / Officers Training Academy / Air Force Academy / Indian Naval Academy
  • CAPF (AC) — Central Armed Police Forces (Assistant Commandant) exam, conducted by UPSC
  • JAG — Judge Advocate General's entry (law graduates)
  • RR — Rashtriya Rifles
  • TA — Territorial Army
  • SF — Special Forces
  • MARCOS — Marine Commando Force (Navy)
  • NSG — National Security Guard
  • BSF / CRPF / ITBP / SSB (force) / CISF — Border Security Force / Central Reserve Police Force / Indo-Tibetan Border Police / Sashastra Seema Bal / Central Industrial Security Force
  • CAPF — Central Armed Police Forces
  • DRDO — Defence Research & Development Organisation
  • HAL / BEL / MDL / GRSE / BDL — Major Defence Public Sector Undertakings (see Defence Industry section)
  • NCC — National Cadet Corps
  • ECHS — Ex-Servicemen Contributory Health Scheme
  • OROP — One Rank One Pension
  • MSP — Military Service Pay

BHARATDEFENCE

Stay Updated

Current Affairs Hub

Verified defence exam notifications and important dates for 2026. Last verified: June 2026. Always cross-check on the official portal before applying — dates can shift.

NDAMay 2026

NDA & NA (II) 2026 Notification Released — 158th Course

UPSC released the NDA & NA (II) 2026 notification on 20 May 2026, for admission to the 158th NDA Course and 120th Indian Naval Academy Course. Written exam is scheduled for 13 September 2026. 12th-pass and appearing candidates are eligible.

Read Official Notice
AFCATMay–Jun 2026

AFCAT 02/2026 Registration Open

Indian Air Force opened AFCAT 02/2026 registration on 20 May 2026, closing 19 June 2026. Exam expected around August 2026. Fee is ₹550 + GST. NCC Special Entry and GATE-score candidates apply without exam fee, via separate entry tabs.

Apply / Check Portal
CDSMay 2026

CDS (II) 2026 Notification — Graduate Entry

UPSC's CDS (II) 2026 notification opens direct commissioned-officer entry into IMA, OTA, INA and AFA for graduates. Note: NDA-I and CDS-I 2026 fell on the same day (12 April 2026) — plan carefully if eligible for both cycles.

Read Official Notice
CAPF (AC)2026

CAPF (AC) 2026 — 349 Vacancies Across 5 Forces

Assistant Commandant vacancies: BSF (108), CRPF (106), CISF (70), ITBP (12), SSB (53). Age 20–25 years as on 1 August 2026, bachelor's degree in any stream. Selection: Written (Paper I & II) → PET → Medical → Interview.

Read Official Notice
AGNIVEER2026

Agniveer Vayu & Army Rallies — Ongoing

Air Force Agniveer Vayu intakes and Army Agniveer recruitment rallies continue through 2026 across states. Eligibility and exam windows vary by intake batch — check the respective official portal for your state/zone schedule.

Rally Schedule

Set Your Own Reminder

Add a personal exam date or form deadline. It'll show a countdown here, and (if you allow notifications) an in-browser alert when it's close.

How to Actually Stay Updated (Beyond This Page)

BharatDefence is a static resource — it can't push live alerts. Build these habits instead:

  • 📰 Read one newspaper (The Hindu / Indian Express) daily — focus on the National & Defence sections.
  • 🔔 Bookmark and check upsc.gov.in, afcat.cdac.in weekly during notification season (Jan–Feb, May–Jun).
  • 📝 Maintain a monthly current-affairs notebook — revise it weekly, not just before the exam.
  • 📲 Follow PIB Defence Wing for verified government announcements instead of unverified social media.
BHARATDEFENCE

Common Questions

Frequently Asked Questions

Yes. Following the Supreme Court of India's ruling in 2021, female candidates are now eligible to apply for NDA. They can join the Army, Navy (excluding submarines), and Air Force (excluding fighter stream, subject to medical standards).

SSB (Services Selection Board) conducts personality assessments for the Army and Navy. AFSB (Air Force Selection Board) conducts the same type of 5-day assessment for Air Force entries via AFCAT. The process is nearly identical — both evaluate OLQs over the same test structure — but are administered by different boards under respective services.

There is no official limit on the number of NDA attempts set by UPSC. However, the age limit (16.5 to 19.5 years) effectively limits most candidates to a maximum of 4–6 attempts since the exam is held twice a year.

Minimum height for Army: 157.5 cm (general), relaxations exist for candidates from certain states/regions. For Navy: 157 cm. For Air Force (flying branch): 162.5 cm to 196 cm. Female candidates have different standards. Height requirements also vary by branch and role.

Agniveers serve for 4 years. At the end of the tenure, 25% are retained as regular cadre based on performance, merit, and organisational requirement. The remaining 75% receive a Seva Nidhi corpus of approximately Rs. 11.71 lakh (tax-free), a skill certificate, and priority in state government/paramilitary jobs as announced by several state governments and the MHA.

Yes. Multiple entries exist for engineering graduates: CDS (IMA/AFA/INA), TGC (Technical Graduate Course — Indian Army), SSC Tech (Short Service Commission — Technical), UES (University Entry Scheme — during final year), AFCAT (Air Force), and various Navy technical entries. SSB Interview is common to all.

Fill your PIQ form thoughtfully and honestly. Know your life history — school, sports, hobbies, family, achievements. Stay updated on defence news, borders, military technology. Practice speaking clearly under mild pressure. Read about Indian military history and current operations. Be consistent — your interview answers should match what you wrote in the PIQ and Self Description.

Before attending SSB, ideally you should be able to: run 2.4 km in under 11 minutes, do 20+ push-ups, 10+ pull-ups, and 30+ sit-ups. Physical fitness is observed during GTO outdoor tasks (PGT, HGT, Individual Obstacles) and while the board does not grade on physical tests directly, obvious lack of fitness reflects negatively on stamina and determination OLQs.
